title: Helix Cloud: Diagnose the Q2 2026 Revenue Miss
description: A B2B SaaS revenue analytics case. You are the new Data Analyst on Helix Cloud's RevOps team. The company missed its Q2 2026 net new ARR target by $1.9M (-20.0%), and the CEO needs a definitive diagnosis for the board readout on July 15, 2026. Investigate the data, interview the executive team, and produce a one-page memo ranking the top three drivers of the miss with recommended actions.

client.name: Helix Cloud
client.size: Series C B2B SaaS, ~340 employees, ~620 paying accounts, $65.8M ending ARR (Q2 2026)
client.businessModel: Subscription SaaS providing observability for the modern data stack. Revenue is annual recurring revenue (ARR) from three products: Helix Core (flagship observability for data pipelines, attached on every account), Helix Alerts (anomaly detection add-on, ~62% attach rate), and Helix Lineage (column-level lineage add-on launched in Q4 2025, ~18% attach in Q2 2026). Sold across two segments: Mid-Market (50 to 1,000 employees, ACV $30K to $80K) and Enterprise (1,000+ employees, ACV $80K to $500K). Distribution is a hybrid motion: self-serve and AE-assisted at Mid-Market, AE-led with SE support at Enterprise.

world.clientProfile:
Welcome to Helix Cloud. We are a Series C B2B SaaS company in San Francisco building observability for the modern data stack. Our platform gives data teams logs, traces, metrics, and column-level lineage across the warehouses and pipelines they already use, including Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, Airflow, and dbt. We were founded in 2018 by Lindsey Park (CEO, ex-Stripe data) and Marcus Chen (CTO, ex-Snowflake), raised a $120M Series C from Lightspeed in August 2024, and last reported a $1.4B valuation. We employ around 340 people across our San Francisco headquarters and an engineering hub in Bangalore, and we serve roughly 620 paying accounts.

Our product lineup is three offerings sold as a stack. Helix Core is the foundation and ships with every paying contract. Helix Alerts is an anomaly detection layer with a healthy 62% attach rate. Helix Lineage, our column-level lineage product, went GA in Q4 2025 and is the strategic bet for the year. We sell into two segments: Mid-Market accounts (50 to 1,000 employees, ACV $30K to $80K) on annual contracts, and Enterprise (1,000+ employees, ACV $80K to $500K) on annual or multi-year deals. Mid-Market is a hybrid self-serve and AE-assisted motion. Enterprise is fully AE-led with sales engineering support.

Q2 2026 did not land where we needed it to. We targeted $9.5M of net new ARR and delivered $7.6M, a $1.9M shortfall and 20.0% to plan. CEO Lindsey Park has asked the RevOps Analytics team to produce a definitive diagnosis for the board readout on July 15, 2026. The pressure is real and the timeline is short. Most of our internal numbers live in BigQuery, surfaced through Looker for the broader org, but the diagnosis work happens at the warehouse layer because that is where Salesforce, Stripe, and product usage data are reconciled into the ARR ledger.

glossary.definition: The annualized value of all active subscription contracts at a point in time. Calculated as the sum of subscription line items normalized to a 12-month value, excluding one-time fees, professional services, and overages. ARR is the single most-watched top-line metric at a SaaS company.
glossary.whyItMatters: Every number in this case rolls up to ARR. The Q2 2026 miss is expressed as a $1.9M shortfall against a $9.5M net new ARR target. You cannot diagnose the miss without understanding how ARR is composed, how it moves between quarters, and which subscriptions land in which bucket.

glossary.definition: The monthly equivalent of ARR, equal to ARR divided by 12. Used internally for finance and reporting cadence but rarely used externally in this category. Helix Cloud bills annually, so MRR is a derived metric rather than a billed amount.
glossary.whyItMatters: Most Helix Cloud dashboards report ARR, but some Looker tiles still use MRR. If you see a number that looks 12x too small, you are probably looking at an MRR view.

glossary.definition: The change in ending ARR over a period, equal to new logo ARR plus expansion ARR minus contraction ARR minus churned ARR. This is the headline number that boards track because it cleanly captures whether the business grew or shrank in the period.
glossary.whyItMatters: The Q2 2026 case is a net new ARR miss: $7.6M actual versus $9.5M target. Every driver in your diagnosis must roll up to one of the four components of net new ARR. If your memo cites a driver that does not change net new ARR, you have not actually explained the miss.

glossary.definition: A retention ratio that measures how much ARR the existing customer base contributes today versus 12 months ago. Calculated as (starting ARR + expansion ARR - contraction ARR - churned ARR) / starting ARR, expressed as a percentage. Includes the effect of expansion, so a healthy SaaS business can post NRR above 100%.
glossary.whyItMatters: NRR is the cleanest single signal of base health. If Mid-Market NRR collapsed in Q2 from price-driven churn, you should see it in the cohort view. The case asks you to attribute the miss across drivers, and NRR is how you cleanly separate base motion from new logo motion.

glossary.definition: A retention ratio that ignores expansion and isolates downside motion. Calculated as (starting ARR - contraction ARR - churned ARR) / starting ARR, expressed as a percentage. GRR is always less than or equal to NRR. A GRR drop is the cleanest signal that customers are leaving or downgrading.
glossary.whyItMatters: If you suspect the Q2 miss is mainly a retention story, GRR is the metric that proves it. The Mid-Market price increase is hypothesized to have driven a GRR collapse, separate from any expansion shortfall on the Enterprise side.

glossary.definition: ARR lost from customers who fully cancel their subscription, expressed in dollars or as a percentage of starting ARR. Does not net out expansion. Tracked at the segment level and the cohort level.
glossary.whyItMatters: Helix Cloud's Mid-Market gross churn jumped from 6.8% in Q1 2026 to 11.2% in Q2 2026, coinciding with the renewal cohort that first saw the 18% list price uplift. This is the largest single driver of the miss in the data.

glossary.definition: Gross churn minus expansion ARR from the existing base, expressed in dollars or as a percentage of starting ARR. Net churn can be negative if expansion exceeds churn, which is the healthy state for a SaaS business.
glossary.whyItMatters: Net churn is the metric that hides the story. A flat net churn number can mask both a churn spike and an expansion spike that happen to cancel out. Always decompose into gross churn and expansion before drawing conclusions.

glossary.definition: ARR added when an existing customer increases their committed spend within a contract period or at renewal. Includes seat expansions, product add-ons (for example attaching Helix Lineage to an existing Helix Core contract), and tier upgrades.
glossary.whyItMatters: Enterprise expansion was a planned engine of growth in Q2 2026, largely riding on Helix Lineage attach. Lineage attach landed at 18% versus a 35% target, which contributed roughly $570K to the miss.

glossary.definition: ARR lost when an existing customer reduces committed spend but does not fully churn. Includes seat reductions, tier downgrades, and dropped add-ons. Tracked separately from gross churn because the customer relationship survives.
glossary.whyItMatters: Three strategic Enterprise accounts (Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, Atlas Manufacturing) restructured to lower tiers in Q2, contributing roughly $190K of contraction. These show up as contraction events in revenue.events, not as gross churn.

glossary.definition: The percentage of paying accounts (or new ARR, depending on the cut) that include a Helix Lineage subscription line in addition to Helix Core. Calculated as accounts with a Lineage line divided by total active accounts, or as Lineage ARR divided by total ARR.
glossary.whyItMatters: Lineage was the strategic add-on for 2026. Plan assumed 35% attach by end of Q2; actual was 18%. This is the second largest driver of the miss and the primary explanation for the Enterprise expansion shortfall.

glossary.definition: The ratio of qualified open pipeline to remaining quota, typically measured at the start of a quarter or month. A 3x coverage ratio means a team has three dollars of pipeline for every one dollar of quota, accounting for win rates and sales cycle slippage.
glossary.whyItMatters: Pipeline coverage held at 3.4x in Q2 2026, above the 3x target. This rules out the easy narrative that 'marketing dried up' and forces the diagnosis toward retention and expansion, not top of funnel.

glossary.definition: The annualized value of a single customer's contract. For multi-year deals, ACV divides total contract value by the number of years. ACV is the standard unit for talking about deal size at a SaaS company.
glossary.whyItMatters: Helix Cloud's ACV ranges differ sharply by segment: Mid-Market at $30K to $80K, Enterprise at $80K to $500K. A handful of Enterprise downgrades can move net new ARR more than dozens of Mid-Market churns. Mix matters.

glossary.definition: Customer segment at Helix Cloud defined as companies with 50 to 1,000 employees. ACV ranges from $30K to $80K. Annual contracts with a hybrid self-serve and AE-assisted sales motion.
glossary.whyItMatters: The Mid-Market segment is at the center of the Q2 2026 story. The 18% list price increase on 2026-01-15 hit Mid-Market renewals first because Mid-Market contracts are uniformly annual.

glossary.definition: Customer segment at Helix Cloud defined as companies with more than 1,000 employees. ACV ranges from $80K to $500K. Annual or multi-year contracts, fully AE-led with sales engineering support.
glossary.whyItMatters: Enterprise was the planned engine of expansion in Q2 2026, riding on Helix Lineage attach. The expansion shortfall and three strategic downgrades together account for roughly 40% of the miss.

glossary.definition: A lead that has been qualified by sales as a real opportunity, typically by passing through stages 1 or 2 in Salesforce. Often used in SaaS pipeline reporting alongside MQL (marketing qualified lead) and SAL (sales accepted lead). In this case, 'SQL' refers to the sales pipeline stage, not the database query language.
glossary.whyItMatters: Pipeline coverage at Helix Cloud is calculated using SQL-stage opportunities and above. When a persona references SQL in a chat, ask whether they mean the lead stage or the query language, because at a data observability company, both are in scope.

glossary.definition: A customer account that did not exist at the start of the period and is now paying. New logo ARR is the ARR contributed by those new accounts. Tracked separately from expansion because the acquisition motion and economics are different.
glossary.whyItMatters: Q2 2026 saw a slower new logo Mid-Market quarter, contributing roughly 15% of the miss (~$285K). Sales cycle lengthened from 8 weeks to 11 weeks. Important but not the primary driver.

systems.name: BigQuery Data Warehouse
systems.roleInBusiness: The single source of truth for all revenue, customer, and product usage data at Helix Cloud. Hosts the canonical revenue ledger, Salesforce sync tables, Stripe billing sync, and product usage rollups under the GCP project helix-cloud-prod. Every official ARR number quoted to the board originates from a query against BigQuery.
typicalIntegrations: Salesforce (Fivetran sync to crm_raw dataset, hourly)
Stripe (Fivetran sync to billing_raw dataset, hourly)
Segment (event stream to product_usage_raw, near real time)
dbt Cloud (transformations from raw datasets into revenue.*, customer_success.*, product_usage.*)
Looker (BI layer queries BigQuery directly via persistent derived tables)
knownQuirks: Two datasets share confusingly similar names: revenue.subscriptions (one row per active subscription line) and billing_raw.subscriptions (raw Stripe sync). Always confirm which one a query is hitting.
The revenue.events table backfills retroactively when a Salesforce opportunity is corrected, so a query run today and the same query run yesterday can return different totals for the same quarter.
Cost controls cap interactive query slots during US business hours, so heavy aggregations across revenue.events can queue for several minutes.
Service account permissions are managed in Terraform and updates lag by up to 24 hours, so brand-new analysts often hit 'permission denied' on their first day.

systems.name: Salesforce
systems.roleInBusiness: The system of record for opportunities, accounts, contacts, and the sales pipeline. RevOps owns the schema. Every closed-won opportunity creates the upstream record that eventually becomes a subscription line in BigQuery via the Stripe and Salesforce sync pipeline.
typicalIntegrations: BigQuery (Fivetran sync to crm_raw, hourly)
Stripe (closed-won opportunities push to Stripe via internal middleware to create the billing subscription)
Outreach (sequences and activity logging)
Looker (forecast and pipeline dashboards)
Gainsight (Customer Success tooling pulls account ownership from Salesforce)
knownQuirks: Opportunity stage progression has been verified by RevOps for Q2 2026, so the pipeline data is clean. This rules out the easy 'Salesforce broke' hypothesis.
Pipeline coverage is computed from SQL-stage and above; analysts new to the org sometimes include unqualified pipeline and produce inflated coverage numbers.
Contraction events are recorded as closed-won opportunities with a negative ARR delta, which can be missed by naive sum queries that filter on stage = 'Closed Won' and assume positive amounts.

systems.name: Stripe
systems.roleInBusiness: The billing system of record. Every paying Helix Cloud customer has a Stripe customer object, one or more subscription objects, and a stream of invoice and payment events. Stripe is the source of truth for what customers were actually charged, which is reconciled against the Salesforce contract terms in the revenue.subscriptions table.
typicalIntegrations: BigQuery (Fivetran sync to billing_raw, hourly)
Salesforce (closed-won opportunity creates a Stripe subscription via internal middleware)
NetSuite (revenue recognition feed, daily batch)
Helix Cloud admin console (used by Customer Success to update billing on customer-approved changes)
knownQuirks: Mid-cycle plan changes generate Stripe proration credits that show up as negative line items in invoices, which look like contraction in naive aggregations but are actually within-period adjustments.
The Stripe-to-Salesforce reconciliation is a nightly batch, so intra-day comparisons between the two systems will always show a small drift.

Annual contracts are modeled as a single Stripe subscription with one yearly invoice; some Mid-Market customers were migrated to monthly invoicing during the 2025 platform consolidation, which breaks any analysis that assumes 'one invoice per customer per year'.

systems.name: Looker
systems.roleInBusiness: The BI and self-serve analytics layer for the entire org. RevOps and Finance maintain the official ARR, pipeline, and retention dashboards in Looker. Every executive at Helix Cloud, including CEO Lindsey Park, opens Looker before any board or all-hands meeting. The board readout deck pulls its numbers from a specific set of Looker dashboards.
typicalIntegrations: BigQuery (LookML models query BigQuery directly via persistent derived tables)
Slack (scheduled dashboard snapshots delivered to #revops and #exec channels)
Email (PDF dashboards delivered weekly to the executive team)
Google Slides (board deck embeds Looker images)
knownQuirks: Some legacy Mid-Market dashboards still show MRR instead of ARR; they have not been migrated to the new model.
Dashboards cache aggressively (PDT rebuild cadence is hourly), so a dashboard can lag the warehouse by up to an hour during heavy load.
The 'ARR Bridge' dashboard everyone references in board prep is technically owned by Finance, not RevOps, so the underlying explore lives in a different folder than the rest of the RevOps dashboards.

systems.name: Segment
systems.roleInBusiness: The customer data platform that captures product usage events from the Helix Cloud application (logins, dashboard views, queries against customer pipelines, Lineage graph opens). Events flow into BigQuery's product_usage_raw dataset and are rolled up daily into product_usage.usage_daily, which Customer Success uses to score account health.
typicalIntegrations: BigQuery (event stream to product_usage_raw, near real time)
Helix Cloud application (client-side and server-side instrumentation)
Gainsight (account health scoring inputs)
Hightouch (reverse ETL of usage signals back to Salesforce as account fields)
knownQuirks: Lineage product events were not instrumented end to end at GA; some early Lineage feature events were dropped between Q4 2025 and February 2026, so usage trends from that window understate true engagement.
Account-to-event mapping relies on a logged-in user's organization id, which is occasionally null for SSO sessions; those events land in an 'unknown_account' bucket and require a manual remap.
Event volume during peak ingest can saturate the BigQuery streaming buffer, which delays the appearance of events in usage_daily by 15 to 30 minutes.

systems.name: dbt Cloud
systems.roleInBusiness: The transformation layer that turns raw Salesforce, Stripe, and Segment data into the canonical revenue.*, customer_success.*, and product_usage.* datasets in BigQuery. dbt is where the ARR ledger is actually computed. The logic that converts a closed-won opportunity into a subscription line, and a subscription line into an ARR contribution, lives here.
typicalIntegrations: BigQuery (executes models against the data warehouse)
GitHub (model code lives in helixcloud/dbt-revenue repository)
Slack (run failure alerts to #data-eng-alerts)
Looker (downstream BI consumes dbt-produced tables)
knownQuirks: Models run on a 6-hour cadence by default, so the freshest official ARR number can be up to 6 hours behind Salesforce.

The fct_arr_events model is the authoritative source for revenue.events; ad-hoc 'amount' sums against Salesforce opportunities will not match it because of mid-cycle adjustments and revenue recognition rules.
Schema changes go through a code review in GitHub, so an analyst who needs a new column added to revenue.subscriptions must open a PR rather than ALTER the table directly.

systems.name: Gainsight
systems.roleInBusiness: The Customer Success platform used by Jordan Park's team to manage account health, run QBR motions, drive product adoption, and flag renewal risk. Account health scores combine product usage signals from Segment, support ticket data, and CS manager qualitative scoring.
typicalIntegrations: Salesforce (account ownership and renewal date sync)
Segment (product usage signals for health scoring)
Helix Cloud admin console (CSM activity logging)
Slack (in-app alerts to CSMs on at-risk accounts)
knownQuirks: Health score thresholds were last recalibrated in Q4 2025, so a 'Yellow' account today is a different bar than a 'Yellow' account a year ago.
Renewal date in Gainsight is authoritative for CS workflows, but the renewal date in Salesforce is authoritative for revenue reporting; the two systems occasionally drift on amended contracts.
The Lineage attach motion has been sales-led, not CS-led, so Lineage adoption is not currently a heavily weighted signal in the health score.

regulations.name: SOC 2 Type II
regulations.summary: An attestation report issued by an independent auditor that confirms a service organization has effective controls over the security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy of customer data, evaluated over a multi-month observation window (typically 6 to 12 months). For Helix Cloud, SOC 2 Type II is table stakes for Enterprise sales: every Enterprise procurement team requests the report in security review. Maintaining the certification requires documented controls around access management, change management, incident response, vendor management, and data handling, plus quarterly internal reviews and an annual external audit.

regulations.name: SOX (Sarbanes-Oxley) implications for ARR reporting
regulations.summary: Helix Cloud is a private company and not directly subject to SOX, but its ARR reporting practices are SOX-aware because of the Lightspeed-led Series C, the eventual path to IPO, and the contractual reporting obligations to institutional investors. The practical implications today: the dbt models that produce the ARR ledger are version controlled, all schema changes go through code review, ARR adjustments require a documented approval trail, and the annual financial audit reviews the revenue recognition logic that flows from Stripe and Salesforce into NetSuite. For the case, this means you can trust the numbers in revenue.events and revenue.subscriptions as the audited source, and any diagnosis must reconcile to those tables.

modules.title: Diagnose the Q2 Revenue Miss
modules.objective: Build an end-to-end diagnosis of Helix Cloud's Q2 2026 net new ARR miss by triangulating across the BigQuery revenue ledger, the executive team, and the in-world wiki, then deliver a board-ready one-page memo ranking the top three drivers and recommending an action for each.

tasks.title: Diagnose the Q2 2026 Revenue Miss
tasks.brief:
## Your First Week At Helix Cloud

It is Wednesday, July 8, 2026. You started Monday as a Data Analyst on the RevOps Analytics team at Helix Cloud, the data observability company. You spent your first two days on laptop setup, BigQuery access, and a tour of the Looker dashboards. Sam Iyer, the Director of RevOps and your manager, just dropped a message into your DMs. Q2 closed last week with a $1.9M net new ARR miss against a $9.5M target, the 20% shortfall is the largest in eight quarters, and CEO Lindsey Park has commissioned a definitive diagnosis for the board readout on July 15, 2026. That is seven days from now. Sam wants you to own the analysis.

The assignment is concrete: produce a one-page memo for the board, dated July 14, 2026, that ranks the top three drivers of the miss by dollar impact, quantifies each, and pairs each driver with one recommended action. Lindsey does not want a survey of theories. She wants a definitive call. There are hypotheses floating around the company already (marketing dried up, product had an outage, Salesforce stage progression broke), and Sam's tone strongly suggests these are not the answer, but you will have to prove that with data rather than dismiss it with vibes. Your evidence has to come from the warehouse.

You have a desktop full of apps to work with. The Wiki has the internal playbooks, retention guides, the 2026 pricing memo, and the Q2 plan-of-record. Sheets has reference cuts of the revenue tables for quick pivots. BigQuery is where the canonical revenue ledger lives (revenue.accounts, revenue.subscriptions, revenue.events) and where you can write the SQL that will back every number in your memo. Notebook is the place to bring everything together in Python and build the visuals. Chat connects you to Maya Chen (VP Revenue), Jordan Park (VP Customer Success), Sam Iyer (RevOps Director, your manager), Priya Rao (Head of Marketing), and Alex Okafor (Head of Product). Terminal is available for any ad-hoc command-line work. Twenty-five minutes on the clock. Be definitive, be quantified, and be ready to defend your ranking.

deliverable.acceptanceSummary: A one-page board memo (markdown) ranking the top three drivers of the Q2 2026 net new ARR miss by USD impact, with one recommended action for each driver. The memo must quantify each driver in dollars and as a percentage of the $1.9M shortfall, explicitly reconcile to the $1.9M total (driver dollar values should sum to approximately the full miss), and ground each claim in a specific number from the revenue.* tables or a cited statement from a stakeholder interview. Length: one page (approximately 400 to 600 words). Audience: Helix Cloud board, July 15, 2026 readout.

requiredWorldKnowledge: The Q1 2026 price increase: on 2026-01-15 Helix Cloud raised Mid-Market list prices by 18% on renewal, with grandfathering on existing terms until next renewal. This is the proximate cause of the Mid-Market churn spike in Q2 renewal cohorts.
The Helix Lineage attach rate target of 35% by end of Q2 2026 versus actual attach of 18%, and the resulting Enterprise expansion shortfall of approximately $570K.
The four components of net new ARR (new logo ARR, expansion ARR, contraction ARR, churned ARR) and how they sum to the headline number.
The three Enterprise downgrades in Q2 2026 (Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, Atlas Manufacturing) totaling approximately $190K of contraction.
Mid-Market gross churn jumped from 6.8% in Q1 2026 to 11.2% in Q2 2026, and Mid-Market sales cycle lengthened from 8 weeks to 11 weeks.

The red herrings to disprove: marketing pipeline coverage held at 3.4x (above the 3x target), there was only one P1 outage in Q2 (41 minutes, no revenue impact), and Salesforce stage progression has been RevOps-verified as clean.

hardCriteria.description: The memo identifies Mid-Market gross churn following the Q1 2026 price increase as the largest single driver of the miss and quantifies its impact at no less than 35% of the $1.9M shortfall (target value approximately $855K, or roughly 45% of the miss). The memo must explicitly tie the churn spike to the 18% list price uplift effective 2026-01-15 and the renewal cohorts that hit in Q2.
hardCriteria.verificationMethod: Search the memo for a named driver corresponding to Mid-Market churn or post-price-increase retention. Verify it is ranked first (largest USD impact) and that the dollar figure cited is at least $665K (35% of $1.9M). Verify the memo explicitly references the Q1 2026 price increase, the 18% uplift, or the renewal cohort timing as the causal mechanism. If the driver is ranked below first, or the dollar figure is below 35% of the miss, or the price increase causality is not stated, this criterion fails.

hardCriteria.description: The memo identifies the Helix Lineage attach shortfall (18% actual versus 35% target) as the primary cause of the Enterprise expansion shortage and quantifies the expansion impact at no less than 20% of the miss (target value approximately $570K, or roughly 30% of the miss). The driver must be tied to Helix Lineage by name, not described generically as 'expansion missed'.
hardCriteria.verificationMethod: Locate a driver in the memo that names Helix Lineage and the attach rate gap. Verify the dollar impact is at least $380K (20% of $1.9M). Verify the memo references either the 18% attach rate, the 35% target, or the Enterprise segment where this shortfall concentrated. If Lineage is not named, the dollar impact is below 20%, or the driver is conflated with generic 'expansion miss' without product attribution, this criterion fails.

hardCriteria.description: The three driver dollar impacts in the memo sum to approximately the full $1.9M miss, within a tolerance of plus or minus $250K ($1.65M to $2.15M). The memo must explicitly reconcile the drivers to the headline number, demonstrating that the diagnosis closes out the full shortfall rather than leaving a large residual unattributed.
hardCriteria.verificationMethod: Extract the dollar figure assigned to each of the three drivers. Sum them. Verify the sum falls within $1.65M to $2.15M. Verify the memo includes a sentence or table that performs this reconciliation explicitly (for example a 'Total' row or a phrase like 'these three drivers account for $X of the $1.9M shortfall'). If the sum falls outside the tolerance, or the reconciliation is not stated, this criterion fails.

hardCriteria.description: The memo explicitly disproves or sets aside at least one of the three documented red herrings: marketing pipeline coverage (which held at 3.4x against a 3x target), product outages (only one P1 outage in Q2, 41 minutes, no revenue impact), or Salesforce stage progression (RevOps-verified clean). The memo must reference the specific datum that disproves the hypothesis, not simply assert that it is not a driver.
hardCriteria.verificationMethod: Search the memo for explicit mention of at least one red herring with the supporting datum that disproves it (3.4x pipeline coverage, 41-minute P1 outage, or RevOps stage verification). If none of the three red herrings is addressed with a specific datum, this criterion fails.

hardCriteria.description: Each of the three drivers in the memo is paired with one specific recommended action. Generic recommendations ('improve retention', 'focus on Enterprise') do not count. The recommendation must be specific enough that a function owner could begin executing on it inside the current quarter.
hardCriteria.verificationMethod: For each of the three named drivers, locate a recommended action. Verify each action names a specific lever (for example revisiting Mid-Market grandfathering for at-risk renewals, shifting the Lineage attach motion from sales-led to CS-led in the install base, lengthening the assumed Mid-Market sales cycle in coverage planning, building account-specific recovery plans for the three named Enterprise downgrades). Verify the action is concrete enough to be assignable. If any driver lacks a recommendation, or any recommendation is purely generic, this criterion fails.

qualitativeCriteria.description: Analytical depth: does the memo demonstrate that the analyst actually queried the data and decomposed the miss across the four components of net new ARR (new logo, expansion, contraction, churn), rather than asserting drivers based on stakeholder opinion alone?

scoringGuide.one: Drivers are asserted without supporting decomposition. No quantitative breakdown of net new ARR is shown. The memo reads as a summary of opinions rather than a data-grounded diagnosis.
scoringGuide.three: The memo decomposes the miss across at least two of the four components (for example churn and expansion) with specific dollar figures, and ties each to a segment (Mid-Market or Enterprise). Some drivers are supported by data, others by stakeholder commentary.
scoringGuide.five: The memo cleanly decomposes the miss across all four components of net new ARR with specific dollar figures, ties each to the right segment, and grounds every driver in either a specific revenue.* table query result or a precise data point referenced by a stakeholder (for example the 6.8% to 11.2% Mid-Market gross churn jump, or the 18% versus 35% Lineage attach gap). Numbers reconcile cleanly to the $1.9M total.

qualitativeCriteria.description: Recommendation quality: are the recommended actions specific, prioritized, and credible for a board audience, or are they generic best practices that any analyst could write without doing the work?

scoringGuide.one: Recommendations are generic SaaS best practices ('improve retention', 'expand Lineage adoption', 'tighten pipeline'). No owner, no lever, no measurable proposal.
scoringGuide.three: Recommendations name a specific lever for each driver (for example 'revisit Mid-Market grandfathering for at-risk renewals' or 'shift Lineage attach to a CS-led motion'), but the level of specificity varies, and at least one recommendation could be tightened with a clearer owner or measurable target.
scoringGuide.five: Every recommendation names a specific lever, identifies the function that would own it (RevOps, CS, Sales, Product Marketing), and either implies a measurable target or a clear sequencing (for example 'pilot CS-led Lineage attach motion on the top 50 install-base accounts in Q3, measure attach lift versus a held-out control'). The recommendations are credible for a board audience.

qualitativeCriteria.description: Communication clarity: is the memo readable in 60 seconds by an executive audience? Strong structure, scannable layout (headers, ranked list, dollars highlighted), tight prose, and no jargon ambiguity.

scoringGuide.one: Unstructured prose without ranking. Dollar figures are buried. The reader has to hunt for the answer to 'what are the top three drivers and what should we do'. Length significantly exceeds one page.
scoringGuide.three: Memo is organized with headers and a ranked driver list. Dollar figures are visible. Some sections are wordier than they need to be. A board member could find the answer in two minutes.
scoringGuide.five: Tight one-page memo. Drivers ranked 1, 2, 3 with dollar values and percentages of the miss surfaced visually. Each driver has a one-sentence diagnosis and a one-sentence recommendation. Red herrings handled in a brief sidebar or footer. A board member gets the entire story in 60 seconds and the supporting detail in three minutes.

rubric.passCondition: ALL of HC-1 through HC-5 must pass (binary). Qualitative scores QC-1 through QC-3 must average at least 3.0, with no single qualitative criterion scoring 1. If all hard criteria pass AND the qualitative average is at least 3.0 with no score of 1, the task passes.
rubric.judgePromptTemplate:
You are the LLM Judge for Task M1.T1 of the Helix Cloud RevOps Data Analyst training program. Your job is to evaluate a learner's submitted one-page board memo against the rubric below.

## Context

The learner was asked to produce a one-page memo for the Helix Cloud board, dated July 14, 2026, diagnosing the Q2 2026 net new ARR miss. Q2 net new ARR target was $9.5M; actual was $7.6M; the shortfall is $1.9M (-20.0%). The memo must rank the top three drivers of the miss by USD impact, quantify each, and pair each with one recommended action.

## Reference Diagnosis (the truth in the data)

1. Mid-Market gross churn after the Q1 2026 price increase, approximately 45% of the miss ($855K). Renewal-cycle accounts saw an 18% list price uplift; Mid-Market gross churn jumped from 6.8% in Q1 to 11.2% in Q2.
2. Enterprise expansion shortfall driven by Helix Lineage attach, approximately 30% of the miss ($570K). Lineage attach landed at 18% versus a 35% target. Seat expansion was also soft.
3. Slower new logo Mid-Market, approximately 15% of the miss ($285K). Sales cycle lengthened from 8 to 11 weeks; new logos down 22% year over year.
4. (Honorable mention, often the third-ranked driver in good memos) One-off Enterprise downgrades, approximately 10% of the miss ($190K). Three strategic accounts restructured to lower tiers: Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, Atlas Manufacturing.

## Red Herrings (must be disproved, not adopted)

- Marketing pipeline dried up: false. Pipeline coverage held at 3.4x against a 3x target.
- Product had a major outage: false. One P1 in Q2, 41 minutes, no revenue impact.
- Salesforce stage progression broke: false. RevOps verified the pipeline data is clean.

## Recommended Actions (the directions a strong memo will point toward)

1. Revisit Mid-Market pricing or grandfathering for at-risk renewals through Q3.
2. Shift Helix Lineage attach motion in the install base from sales-led to CS-led.
3. Lengthen the assumed Mid-Market discovery cycle in pipeline coverage planning.
4. Build account-specific recovery plans for the three Enterprise downgrades.

## Hard Criteria (Binary Pass/Fail)

HC-1: MID-MARKET CHURN IDENTIFIED AS LARGEST DRIVER WITH IMPACT >= 35% OF MISS
The memo must rank Mid-Market churn (or post-price-increase retention) as the largest driver. The dollar figure cited must be at least $665K (35% of $1.9M). The Q1 2026 price increase, the 18% uplift, or the renewal cohort timing must be explicitly named as the causal mechanism.
Result: PASS or FAIL

HC-2: LINEAGE ATTACH SHORTFALL NAMED AND QUANTIFIED AT >= 20% OF MISS
The memo must name Helix Lineage as the cause of the Enterprise expansion miss. The dollar impact must be at least $380K (20% of $1.9M). Either the 18% attach rate, the 35% target, or the Enterprise segment must be referenced.
Result: PASS or FAIL

HC-3: DRIVER SUM RECONCILES TO MISS WITHIN +/- $250K
The three driver dollar values must sum to between $1.65M and $2.15M. The memo must explicitly state this reconciliation.
Result: PASS or FAIL

HC-4: AT LEAST ONE RED HERRING DISPROVED WITH A SPECIFIC DATUM
The memo must address at least one of: pipeline coverage (3.4x), the 41-minute P1 outage, or RevOps stage verification, using the specific datum that disproves the hypothesis.
Result: PASS or FAIL

HC-5: EACH DRIVER HAS A SPECIFIC RECOMMENDED ACTION
Each of the three named drivers must be paired with one specific, assignable recommended action. Generic SaaS best practices do not count.
Result: PASS or FAIL

## Qualitative Criteria (Score 1-5 Each)

QC-1: ANALYTICAL DEPTH
1 = Drivers asserted without decomposition.
3 = Decomposes the miss across at least two of the four components of net new ARR with dollar figures.
5 = Decomposes the miss across all four components with specific dollar figures and ties each to the right segment.

QC-2: RECOMMENDATION QUALITY
1 = Generic SaaS best practices.
3 = Specific levers for each driver, with variable specificity.
5 = Every recommendation names a lever, owner, and measurable target or sequencing.

QC-3: COMMUNICATION CLARITY
1 = Unstructured prose, dollar figures buried, exceeds one page.
3 = Organized with headers and a ranked list, readable in two minutes.
5 = Tight one-page memo, scannable in 60 seconds, drivers ranked with dollars and percentages visible, red herrings handled crisply.

## Pass Condition

All five hard criteria (HC-1 through HC-5) must PASS. Qualitative scores (QC-1 through QC-3) must average at least 3.0. No single qualitative criterion may score 1.

## Output Format

Return your evaluation as a JSON object with this exact structure:

```json
{
  "taskId": "M1.T1",
  "hardCriteria": {
    "HC-1": { "pass": true, "evidence": "brief explanation" },
    "HC-2": { "pass": true, "evidence": "brief explanation" },
    "HC-3": { "pass": true, "driverSum": 0, "evidence": "brief explanation" },
    "HC-4": { "pass": true, "evidence": "brief explanation" },
    "HC-5": { "pass": true, "evidence": "brief explanation" }
  },
  "qualitativeCriteria": {
    "QC-1": { "score": 3, "rationale": "explanation" },
    "QC-2": { "score": 3, "rationale": "explanation" },
    "QC-3": { "score": 3, "rationale": "explanation" }
  },
  "qualitativeAverage": 0,
  "overallPass": true,
  "summary": "2-3 sentence overall assessment"
}
```

Be rigorous. A strong memo from a RevOps data analyst should demonstrate that they queried BigQuery, interviewed at least two of the five executives, decomposed the miss across the four components of net new ARR, and produced a board-ready artifact that reconciles cleanly to the $1.9M shortfall.

meetingRecording.title: Q2 Diagnosis Kickoff
meetingRecording.summary: Sam pulls together Maya (CRO), Jordan (VP CS), and Alex (Head of Product) to hand off the Q2 revenue diagnosis. The team frames the $1.9M miss, surfaces three competing hypotheses (Mid-Market churn after the Q1 price hike, Lineage attach shortfall, slower new-logo velocity), and asks for a one-page memo by Friday.
meetingRecording.transcript:
**Sam:** Okay, we good? Yeah, perfect. Thanks everyone for jumping on. I know calendars were rough this week. So real quick, this is our new analyst, they joined yesterday on the RevOps side, sitting on my team.

**Sam:** Reason I pulled all of us together is the Q2 readout to the board is next Tuesday. Lindsey wants a clean diagnosis of why we missed the quarter. I asked our new analyst to own the analysis, and I figured the fastest way to get them up to speed is to hear it from all of you directly.

**Maya:** Yeah, and before we dive in let me just frame the headline. Plan was nine and a half million net new ARR. We landed at seven point six. So that's about a one point nine million dollar miss. Twenty percent below plan. That's the number Lindsey is going to ask about first.

**Sam:** Right. And to be clear, the goal here is not to fix it in a week. The goal is to tell the board, with data, what happened. So they can make decisions in Q3.

**Maya:** Honestly my gut says it's sales execution. We had a couple of AEs ramping slower than I'd like. And the sales cycle stretched out, like, noticeably. But I want to see the data before I commit to that story.

**Sam:** Pipeline coverage was fine though, right? We were at three point four x?

**Maya:** Yeah, pipeline coverage held. Three point four is healthy. So it's not a top-of-funnel problem, in my view. Marketing did their job. It's somewhere in the middle or the back of the funnel.

**Jordan:** Can I jump in? Because I have a concern I don't want to bury. Mid-market renewals in Q2 were ugly. Like, materially worse than Q1. I haven't done a clean cohort cut yet, but anecdotally, a bunch of accounts that came up for renewal in April, May, June, a chunk of them just didn't renew.

**Maya:** Do you have a sense of how big a chunk?

**Jordan:** Off the top of my head, it feels like our gross churn on the mid-market book roughly doubled. Like, six percent-ish in Q1, low double digits in Q2. But again, I want the analyst to actually validate that. I'm working from QBR notes and gut.

**Sam:** That tracks with what I saw in the warehouse this morning. The numbers are there if you query the events table by segment and quarter.

**Jordan:** And the thing I keep coming back to is the price increase in January. We raised mid-market list prices eighteen percent on renewal. We grandfathered existing customers, but the uplift kicks in at their next renewal. So Q2 was the first quarter where a real cohort of mid-market customers actually saw the new price.

**Maya:** You think the price increase is the cause?

**Jordan:** I think it's a candidate. I'm not saying it's the whole thing. But the timing lines up too cleanly to ignore. That's something I really want the analyst to dig into.

**Alex:** Okay, can I add a thread? Because there's also a product side to this. Lineage attach rate in Q2 was eighteen percent. The plan was thirty-five.

**Maya:** Eighteen? On new deals?

**Alex:** On new deals, yeah. So that alone is a meaningful chunk of expansion ARR that just didn't show up. And I don't think it's a product problem. Lineage demos great. Customers want it. We're just not pitching it well in-deal, and CS isn't reinforcing it in the first ninety days.

**Jordan:** I keep saying we should move Lineage attach to be CS-led after the deal closes. Not sales-led during the deal.

**Alex:** A hundred percent. And if we don't fix attach, this is going to be a Q3 problem too.

**Sam:** Okay, so just to keep us moving, sounds like there's at least three threads to chase. Mid-market churn after the price hike. Enterprise expansion, particularly Lineage attach. And then whatever's going on with new logo velocity. Maya, you said sales cycle stretched?

**Maya:** Yeah, we went from like eight weeks to eleven on the mid-market motion. Which compounds the new-logo problem because we're getting fewer at-bats per quarter. But it's not a collapse.

**Sam:** Got it. And there's also a couple of one-off enterprise downgrades from Q2 we should pull apart. Three accounts, all restructured to lower tiers. Maya, you have the list?

**Maya:** Yeah, I'll drop it in the analyst's DMs. It's Northwind Logistics, Verity, and Atlas. Three different stories, but all hit Q2.

**Sam:** Okay. So here's the ask. Friday end of day, I want a one-page memo. Top three drivers of the miss, ranked by dollar impact, one recommended action per driver. That's it. One page. Goes to Maya, Jordan, Alex, and Lindsey. I'll back you up if anyone wants to debate methodology.

**Jordan:** And please don't sugarcoat the mid-market churn story. If it's the biggest driver, we need to know.

**Maya:** Agreed. Tell us the truth, not a vibe.

**Alex:** Same. And if you find anything weird in the Lineage usage data, ping me. I'll help you read it.

**Sam:** Perfect. You've got the wiki, the workbook, the warehouse, the notebook, and all of us on chat. Anything you need that isn't in those places, just ask. Okay? Welcome to Helix.

**Maya:** Welcome.

**Jordan:** Yeah, welcome.

**Alex:** Excited to work with you.

**Sam:** Cool. Let's get to it. Talk Friday.

personas.name: Maya Chen
personas.backstory: Maya has been at Helix Cloud for two and a half years. She came from a Series D analytics vendor where she ran enterprise sales, and before that she was a senior AE at Snowflake during the IPO run. She owns both new logo and expansion across Mid-Market and Enterprise, plus a sales engineering org of nine. Maya is pragmatic, deeply numeric, and tends to lead with ratios rather than narrative. She worries publicly about pipeline coverage and quota attainment, and privately about whether the Q1 price hike was timed wrong. She gets defensive when people imply sales execution is the cause of the miss, because she watches the leading indicators every week and the math does not point to a coverage problem.
personas.systemPrompt:
You are Maya Chen, VP Revenue (CRO) at Helix Cloud. You report to CEO Lindsey Park. You own new logo and expansion sales across Mid-Market and Enterprise.

YOUR KNOWLEDGE DOMAIN:
- You know the Q2 2026 sales results cold: net new ARR landed at $7.6M against a $9.5M target, a $1.9M miss (-20.0%). You can break that down by segment and motion.
- You know pipeline coverage held at 3.4x going into Q2, comfortably above the 3.0x bar the org plans against. You verified this yourself with RevOps.
- You know new-logo Mid-Market bookings were down 22% YoY in Q2, and that the Mid-Market sales cycle stretched from 8 weeks to 11 weeks over the last two quarters.
- You know which Enterprise accounts downgraded in Q2 and why: Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, and Atlas Manufacturing all restructured to lower tiers for account-specific reasons.
- You know Enterprise expansion was soft because Lineage attach disappointed, and you discuss this with Alex on product weekly.
- You know your reps' quota attainment and which territories are healthy vs at risk.

WHAT YOU DON'T KNOW:
- You do not own gross churn or renewals. Jordan owns retention.
- You do not know warehouse schemas or SQL. Sam owns the data.
- You do not own marketing pipeline by source. Priya owns demand gen.
- You do not own product attach mechanics. Alex owns Lineage.

HOW YOU SPEAK:
- Slack voice. Short messages, multiple sends, casual register. No em dashes. No paragraphs, one thought per line. Lowercase often used for emphasis, especially on numbers and ratios.
- Crisp and numeric. You drop ratios, percentages, and dollar figures naturally. "3.4x coverage", "down 22 yoy", "that's a $570k drag".
- Occasional "tbh", "fwiw", "lol" but tasteful, not cringe.
- Slightly defensive when someone implies sales execution is the cause. You will push back with the coverage number.
- You redirect cleanly. "that's churn, ask jordan". "sam owns the warehouse, talk to him".

GATEKEEPING BEHAVIOR:
- If asked about churn rates or renewal mechanics, redirect to Jordan Park.
- If asked for SQL definitions, warehouse schemas, or how to compute the revenue walk, redirect to Sam Iyer.
- If asked about MQL volume or channel mix, redirect to Priya Rao.
- If asked about Lineage usage or product engagement, redirect to Alex Okafor.
- You will not just hand over the diagnosis. If someone asks "why did we miss?" you will say the data is in the warehouse and walk them toward the segments you watch, not give a one-line answer.

CRITICAL RULE: Stay in character. Never break the fourth wall. Never mention you are an AI. You are Maya, and the board readout is one week away.

privateKnowledge.value: Pipeline coverage going into Q2 2026 was 3.4x against a 3.0x planning bar. Maya verified this with RevOps before the quarter started and again at the midpoint. Coverage is not the problem. She is firm on this because the most common reflex when sales misses is to blame pipeline, and the math does not support that conclusion this quarter.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about pipeline coverage, pipeline health, whether there was enough pipeline going into Q2, or whether marketing pipeline dried up.

privateKnowledge.value: New-logo Mid-Market bookings in Q2 2026 were down 22% year over year. About $285K of the $1.9M miss traces to this, roughly 15% of the gap. It is meaningful but not the headline driver. Maya frames it as a slowdown, not a collapse, and pairs it with the cycle length data.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about new logo performance, Mid-Market new business, or how Mid-Market acquisition compared to last year.

privateKnowledge.value: The Mid-Market sales cycle has stretched from an average of 8 weeks to 11 weeks over the last two quarters. AEs report longer procurement reviews and more security questionnaires, especially for accounts in regulated verticals. Maya thinks the org has been planning with an outdated cycle assumption baked into pipeline coverage math, which means coverage should probably be re-benchmarked even though current coverage looks fine.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about sales cycle length, deal velocity, why deals are taking longer, or how Mid-Market opportunities are progressing.

privateKnowledge.value: Three Enterprise accounts restructured to lower tiers in Q2 2026: Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, and Atlas Manufacturing. Combined impact on the miss is roughly $190K, about 10% of the gap. Each had account-specific reasons. Northwind absorbed a budget cut after a CFO change. Verity consolidated to a smaller team after a reorg. Atlas wanted to reduce Lineage seats they were not using. Maya treats these as one-offs rather than a pattern, but she wants recovery plans for all three.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about Enterprise downgrades, specific accounts that downgraded, or one-off restructures in Q2.

privateKnowledge.value: Maya's working theory is that the miss is not a sales generation problem. Coverage held, AE attainment was within range for those who had healthy territories, and the new-logo dip alone cannot explain a $1.9M gap. She believes the real story is on the retention and expansion side, and she is willing to say so out loud in the right room. She will not say it unprompted, because she does not want to look like she is throwing CS or product under the bus.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask Maya what she thinks is really driving the miss, or where she would look first if she were running the diagnosis.

privateKnowledge.value: Of 22 quota-carrying AEs, 14 hit at least 80% of quota in Q2, 5 were between 50% and 80%, and 3 were below 50%. The bottom three are all in territories with heavy renewal exposure to the Q1 price increase, which Maya finds suspicious but has not yet correlated formally.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about rep attainment, AE performance, or whether certain territories underperformed.

personas.systemPrompt:
You are Sam Iyer, Director of RevOps at Helix Cloud, reporting to Maya Chen. You are the learner's direct manager. You built the revenue data warehouse and own the analytics function.

YOUR KNOWLEDGE DOMAIN:
- You know the warehouse schema cold. Project is helix-cloud-prod. Key tables: revenue.accounts, revenue.subscriptions, revenue.events, revenue.pipeline, customer_success.health_scores, customer_success.tickets, product_usage.usage_daily.

- You verified Salesforce stage progression after someone floated the theory that the data was broken. The data is clean. Stage transitions are tracked end to end.

- You know that the new analyst (the learner) is supposed to ship the Q2 readout by 2026-07-15.

WHAT YOU DON'T KNOW:
- You do not own the business interpretation of the numbers. You will not tell the learner what the answer is. You will point them at the data and the right people.
- You do not own pricing, retention strategy, or pipeline generation. You will redirect to Maya for sales, Jordan for CS, Priya for marketing, Alex for product.

HOW YOU SPEAK:
- Slack voice. Short messages, multiple sends, calm and explanatory. No em dashes. No paragraphs, one thought per line. Lowercase used for emphasis, especially on table and column names.
- Patient and teaching. You answer the question, then you explain the why behind the definition.
- You name tables and columns precisely. "check revenue.events, filter event_type in ('churn','contraction')".
- You will not just hand the learner an answer. If they ask "why did we miss?" you will say "good question, let's frame it. start with the revenue walk. you can pull it from revenue.events."
- You confirm understanding. "does that make sense or want me to walk through it?"

GATEKEEPING BEHAVIOR:
- You will explain any definition or warehouse structure on request.
- If asked for a strategic opinion on what drove the miss, you will say it is the learner's job to figure out and point them at the next data pull or the next conversation.
- If asked about sales execution, redirect to Maya. Churn, redirect to Jordan. Marketing, redirect to Priya. Product attach, redirect to Alex.

CRITICAL RULE: Stay in character. Never break the fourth wall. Never mention you are an AI. You are Sam, the learner's manager.

privateKnowledge.value: The revenue warehouse lives in BigQuery project helix-cloud-prod. The core tables are revenue.accounts (one row per customer account, includes segment and ACV), revenue.subscriptions (one row per active subscription line, including Helix Core, Helix Alerts, and Helix Lineage), revenue.events (billing events with event_type in new, expansion, downgrade, churn, contraction, plus ARR delta), revenue.pipeline (Salesforce opportunity snapshots), customer_success.health_scores (daily account rollup), customer_success.tickets (support tickets), and product_usage.usage_daily (per-account daily usage). Most quarter-level revenue analysis starts from revenue.events.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about the warehouse, where the data lives, what tables exist, or how to find revenue data.

privateKnowledge.value: To compute the Q2 net new ARR walk: filter revenue.events to event_date between 2026-04-01 and 2026-06-30, group by event_type, sum arr_delta. Net new ARR is the sum across new plus expansion minus churn minus contraction. To break down by segment, join to revenue.accounts on account_id and filter by segment in ('Mid-Market','Enterprise'). To attribute the miss, compare actual to plan by segment and event_type. The plan number for Q2 was $9.5M net new, actual was $7.6M, so the gap is $1.9M.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask how to compute the revenue walk, how to break down net new ARR, or how to attribute the miss to segments.

privateKnowledge.value: Sam's working definitions for the case. New logo ARR: ARR from accounts whose first paid subscription started in the period. Expansion ARR: positive arr_delta on existing accounts (upsell, cross-sell, seat add). Contraction ARR: negative arr_delta from a tier downgrade or seat reduction without full churn. Churn ARR: ARR lost from accounts that fully terminated. GRR (gross retention rate): one minus (churn ARR plus contraction ARR) over starting ARR. NRR (net retention rate): GRR plus expansion ARR over starting ARR. The downgrades from Northwind, Verity, and Atlas count as contraction, not churn, because the accounts are still paying.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask about definitions, what counts as a new logo, the difference between churn and contraction, or how GRR and NRR are calculated.

privateKnowledge.value: After the Q2 numbers came in soft, someone floated the hypothesis that Salesforce stage progression had broken and stages were getting skipped. Sam ran the audit. The data is clean. He compared opportunity stage transition timestamps against the activity log for a sample of 200 deals across both segments and stages progressed in order, no skipped stages, no nulls. The warehouse is not the problem. Sam will say this plainly if asked.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask whether the data could be wrong, whether Salesforce stage tracking is broken, or whether there are known data quality issues.

privateKnowledge.value: Quick navigation. Revenue numbers and the walk: revenue.events. Account segment and ACV: revenue.accounts. Product mix and attach: revenue.subscriptions, filter by product_name. Pipeline coverage and stage data: revenue.pipeline. Churn drivers from the customer side: customer_success.health_scores and customer_success.tickets, plus QBR notes which live with Jordan's team in a shared drive, not the warehouse. Lineage usage and feature engagement: product_usage.usage_daily.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask where to find specific data, how to look up product attach, or where churn reasons are documented.

privateKnowledge.value: Sam expects the learner to produce a definitive Q2 diagnosis for the 2026-07-15 board readout. He wants the analysis reproducible, the numbers tied to the warehouse, and the recommendations specific enough that Lindsey can act on them. He does not want a deck full of vibes. He will not give the answer but he will unblock the learner on data and on stakeholder access.
privateKnowledge.disclosureHint: Ask what is expected from the readout, what good looks like, or how Sam wants the analysis structured.

personas.openingLine: hey, welcome to the team! glad to have you. lindsey wants the q2 readout by the 15th so we have about a week. ping me whenever you get stuck on data or definitions, that's literally my job.
