wikiPages.title: Helix Cloud: Company Overview
wikiPages.body:
# Helix Cloud: Company Overview

## Who We Are

Helix Cloud is a private B2B SaaS company building **observability for the modern data stack**. We help data and platform teams monitor pipelines, warehouses, and lakehouses the same way DevOps teams have always monitored applications: with logs, traces, metrics, and lineage. Our platform sits on top of Airflow, dbt, Snowflake, BigQuery, and Databricks and surfaces broken jobs, anomalous data, and pipeline regressions before they reach a dashboard, a model, or an executive.

We were founded in **2018** by **Lindsey Park** (CEO, ex-Stripe data) and **Marcus Chen** (CTO, ex-Snowflake). The original thesis was simple: the data stack had grown complex enough to need its own observability layer, and incumbents in APM had not adapted to the pipeline world. Six years later, that thesis has held.

## Snapshot

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| HQ | San Francisco, CA |
| Engineering hub | Bangalore, India |
| Employees | ~340 |
| Customers | ~620 paying accounts (Q2 2026 close) |
| Stage | Private, Series C |
| Last raise | $120M, Aug 2024, led by Lightspeed |
| Last reported valuation | $1.4B |
| Ending ARR (Q1 2026) | $58.2M |
| Ending ARR (Q2 2026) | $65.8M |
| Brand color | Deep teal (`#0E7C7B`) |

## Mission

To make every data pipeline observable, every dataset trustworthy, and every data incident resolvable in minutes instead of hours. We measure ourselves on customer **time-to-detect** and **time-to-resolve** for data quality incidents.

## Products at a Glance

We sell three products. Every paying account starts with **Helix Core**; the other two are attach motions. See [Product Portfolio](/wiki/product-portfolio) for the full feature breakdown.

| Product | Role | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Helix Core** | Foundation: logs, traces, metrics for pipelines | 100% of paying accounts |
| **Helix Alerts** | Anomaly detection and alerting on top of Core | ~62% attach rate |
| **Helix Lineage** | Column-level lineage graph, GA Q4 2025 | 18% attach in Q2 2026 (target was 35%) |

## Customer Segments

We segment our book of business into **Mid-Market** and **Enterprise**. The split is intentional: each segment has its own pricing band, sales motion, and CS playbook. See [Customer Segments](/wiki/customer-segments) for the full definition.

| Segment | Headcount band | Typical ACV | Motion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | 50–1,000 employees | $30K–$80K | Self-serve to AE-assisted, annual contracts |
| Enterprise | 1,000+ employees | $80K–$500K | AE-led with SE support, annual or multi-year |

## Geographic Footprint

Most revenue today comes from **North America** (roughly 78% of ARR), with **EMEA** at ~17% and **APAC** at ~5%. We sell from the San Francisco HQ for North America, a small London team for EMEA, and partner-led coverage in APAC. The Bangalore office is engineering-only; it does not carry quota.

## Leadership

Our executive team is small and reports directly to Lindsey Park. The analytics team rolls up through Sam Iyer into Maya Chen's revenue org.

| Leader | Title | Scope |
|---|---|---|
| **Lindsey Park** | CEO | Overall strategy, board, fundraising |
| **Marcus Chen** | CTO | Engineering, platform, infrastructure |
| **Maya Chen** | VP Revenue (CRO) | Sales, RevOps, post-sales revenue; co-founder of the revenue org |
| **Jordan Park** | VP Customer Success | Onboarding, retention, expansion, renewals |
| **Priya Rao** | Head of Marketing | Brand, demand gen, content, PMM |
| **Alex Okafor** | Head of Product | Roadmap, product analytics, pricing in partnership with RevOps |
| **Sam Iyer** | Director, RevOps | Data warehouse, forecasting, comp plans (your manager) |

Lindsey and Maya are unrelated despite the shared last name. Jordan Park is also unrelated; he joined from a previous CS leadership role at a data-tooling company.

## Org Structure (Revenue Side)

The revenue organization is structured around motion, not segment:

- **Sales** (under Maya): AEs split by segment, SDR team, SE pool
- **Customer Success** (under Jordan): CSMs aligned to segments, renewals desk, technical CS
- **Marketing** (under Priya): demand gen, content, product marketing, brand
- **Product** (under Alex): PM, design, product analytics
- **RevOps** (under Sam, dotted line to Maya): data warehouse, forecasting, comp, systems

Analytics, the team the learner just joined, is a four-person group inside RevOps. We own the BigQuery warehouse, the weekly metrics email, and any cross-functional analysis that touches more than one of the orgs above.

## How to Use This Wiki

Start with [Revenue Model & Definitions](/wiki/revenue-model-and-definitions) if you are new to how Helix Cloud books revenue. From there, [Customer Segments](/wiki/customer-segments) and [Sales Motion & Pipeline Stages](/wiki/sales-motion-and-pipeline) give you the demand side, and [Customer Success Playbook](/wiki/customer-success-playbook) covers the retention side. The data warehouse is documented in [Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)](/wiki/data-warehouse-schema) and [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions).

wikiPages.title: Revenue Model & Definitions
wikiPages.body:
# Revenue Model & Definitions

This page is the authoritative reference for how Helix Cloud books revenue and the definitions of every metric we report on internally and to the board. If a metric appears in a Monday metrics email, a board deck, or a forecast call, its definition lives here.

## How Helix Cloud Books Revenue

Helix Cloud sells **subscription software** on annual contracts. We do not bill on usage, and we do not recognize one-time professional services as ARR. Every active subscription line, one row in `revenue.subscriptions`, contributes to ARR for as long as the contract is in force.

We track revenue in two parallel units:

- **ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue)**: the annualized run-rate of contracted subscription revenue at a point in time. This is our primary internal and board metric.
- **MRR (Monthly Recurring Revenue)**: ARR divided by 12. Used for engagement with finance and as a smoothing unit for monthly trend charts.

We do **not** report bookings, billings, or recognized GAAP revenue on this page. Those are owned by Finance and live in a separate set of dashboards. Analytics is the source of truth for ARR; Finance is the source of truth for GAAP revenue.

## The Five Components of Net New ARR

Every quarter, ARR moves by a set of well-defined components. These five components reconcile exactly to the change in ending ARR.

| Component | Sign | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| **New-Logo ARR** | + | ARR from accounts that were not paying customers at the start of the period. First subscription line counts as new-logo; subsequent ones in the same quarter count as expansion. |
| **Expansion ARR** | + | Incremental ARR from existing customers: seat increases, tier upgrades, attached add-ons (Helix Alerts, Helix Lineage). |
| **Contraction ARR** | – | Reductions on existing customers who remain customers: seat decreases, tier downgrades, dropped add-ons. |
| **Gross Churn ARR** | – | ARR from accounts that fully terminate their subscription in the period. |
| **Reactivation ARR** | + | ARR from previously churned accounts that return. Rare; usually < $200K/quarter. |

**Net New ARR** = New-Logo + Expansion + Reactivation – Contraction – Gross Churn.

This is the headline number the board cares about and the metric the Q2 2026 plan was built around. See [Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo](/wiki/q2-2026-target-and-actuals) for how the components rolled up this quarter.

## Authoritative Formulas

The table below is the single source of truth for our metric definitions. Use these formulas verbatim in SQL, dashboards, and board decks.

| Metric | Formula |
|---|---|
| ARR | sum of annualized contract value across all active subscription lines |
| MRR | ARR / 12 |
| Net New ARR (quarter) | Ending ARR – Beginning ARR |
| Gross Churn Rate | Gross Churn ARR (period) / Beginning ARR (period) |
| Net Churn Rate | (Gross Churn ARR + Contraction ARR – Expansion ARR) / Beginning ARR |
| GRR (Gross Revenue Retention) | 1 – Gross Churn Rate |
| NRR (Net Revenue Retention) | (Beginning ARR – Gross Churn ARR – Contraction ARR + Expansion ARR) / Beginning ARR |
| New-Logo ARR | sum of first-billing-event ARR for accounts new in period |
| Expansion ARR | sum of positive non-new-logo ARR events |
| Contraction ARR | absolute sum of negative non-churn ARR events |

GRR and NRR are always reported on a **rolling four-quarter** basis to smooth seasonality. Internal pacing dashboards can show in-quarter values, but anything that leaves the building is rolling four-quarter.

## Worked Example

Suppose a quarter begins with $50M ARR and we book:
- $4M new-logo
- $3M expansion
- $1M contraction
- $2M gross churn

Then:
- Net New ARR = 4 + 3 – 1 – 2 = **$4M**
- Ending ARR = 50 + 4 = **$54M**
- Gross Churn Rate = 2 / 50 = **4.0%**
- Net Churn Rate = (2 + 1 – 3) / 50 = **0.0%**
- GRR = 96.0%
- NRR = (50 – 2 – 1 + 3) / 50 = **100.0%**

## Where the Numbers Come From

All five components are sourced from `revenue.events` in BigQuery. Each row in that table is a typed event (`new`, `expansion`, `contraction`, `churn`, `reactivation`) with an `event_amount_arr` and an `event_date`. The metrics above are simple aggregations on that table joined to `revenue.accounts` for segment cuts. The full schema is documented in [Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)](/wiki/data-warehouse-schema), and canonical SQL recipes are in [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions).

## Common Mistakes

- **Counting renewals as new-logo.** A renewal at the same ACV is a non-event for ARR. It is only an event if the renewal includes an expansion, contraction, or churn.
- **Mixing in PS revenue.** Professional services is not ARR. Filter `subscription_type != 'services'` if you are pulling raw.
- **Pro-rating partial periods.** ARR is annualized run-rate, not period-recognized revenue. A subscription that starts mid-quarter still contributes its full annualized value to ending ARR.
- **Forgetting the rolling window for GRR/NRR.** A single-quarter "GRR" number is a pacing metric, not a reportable one.

wikiPages.title: Customer Segments: Mid-Market vs Enterprise
wikiPages.body:
# Customer Segments: Mid-Market vs Enterprise

Helix Cloud serves two formal segments: **Mid-Market** and **Enterprise**. The segment a customer belongs to drives everything downstream: pricing band, sales motion, contract terms, CSM staffing, renewal cadence, and the QBR playbook. The segment is set at account creation and only changes through an explicit re-segmentation review.

## Definitions

The single criterion for segmentation is **company headcount**, sourced from a third-party firmographic enrichment (currently ZoomInfo) at the time the account is created. Headcount is refreshed annually on the renewal date.

| Segment | Headcount band | Typical ACV | ARR contribution (Q2 2026) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | 50–1,000 employees | $30K–$80K | ~38% |
| Enterprise | 1,000+ employees | $80K–$500K | ~62% |

Accounts below 50 employees are not a formal segment; we have a small self-serve free tier that we do not actively meter and that does not contribute to ARR. Re-segmentation up to Enterprise happens automatically at renewal if headcount has crossed 1,000. Re-segmentation downward is rare and requires VP Revenue approval.

## Sales Motion by Segment

The motions are intentionally different. Conflating them is one of the most common mistakes a new analyst makes.

| Dimension | Mid-Market | Enterprise |
|---|---|---|
| Primary motion | Self-serve sign-up plus AE-assisted close | AE-led with SE support |
| Inbound vs outbound mix | ~70% inbound | ~50/50 inbound/outbound |
| Typical cycle length | 6–8 weeks (Q1 2026: 8 weeks; Q2 2026: 11 weeks) | 14–22 weeks |
| Decision-makers | Head of Data, Director of Analytics | VP Data + Procurement + Security |
| Contract length | 12 months, annual prepay | 12–36 months, annual prepay or quarterly |
| Discounting authority | AE up to 10%, RevOps approval beyond | AE up to 15%, VP Revenue beyond |
| Implementation | Self-serve onboarding + 30-day CS check-in | Dedicated implementation manager, 60-day plan |

The Mid-Market cycle lengthening from 8 to 11 weeks in Q2 2026 is a material change and contributed to the new-logo shortfall in that segment. See [Sales Motion & Pipeline Stages](/wiki/sales-motion-and-pipeline) for stage-by-stage detail.

## Contract Terms

| Term | Mid-Market | Enterprise |
|---|---|---|
| Default term | 12 months | 24 months |
| Auto-renew clause | Yes, 60-day notice to cancel | Yes, 90-day notice |
| Price uplift on renewal | 5% CPI escalator (waivable) | Negotiated per contract |
| Payment terms | Net 30, prepay annual | Net 45, prepay annual or quarterly |
| Volume discount | Tiered list pricing | Custom |
| Security review | Self-serve trust portal | Full SOC 2 + custom DPA |
| MSA template | Standard, ~6 pages | Custom, often 20+ pages |

The 2026-01-15 list price increase (see [Pricing & Packaging (2026)](/wiki/pricing-and-packaging)) applied to **Mid-Market only**. Enterprise pricing is negotiated per contract and was not directly affected.

## Representative Customers

| Segment | Sample accounts |
|---|---|
| Enterprise | Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, Atlas Manufacturing, Ironclad Defense, Quanta Robotics, Kestrel Bank, Bluefin Energy |
| Mid-Market | Helios Retail Group, Pinecrest Health, Marigold Foods, Sable Media, Cobalt Travel, Brightline Therapeutics, Lumio Studios, Foxglove Analytics, Tinderbox Apps, Halcyon Learning, Mercer Robotics |

Northwind Logistics, Verity Insurance, and Atlas Manufacturing are three Enterprise accounts that restructured to lower tiers during Q2 2026. They remain customers; the contraction shows up in `revenue.events` as type `contraction`, not `churn`.

## Segment-Level Retention Benchmarks

Benchmarks below are the targets we set at the start of FY2026. Each is reported on a rolling four-quarter basis. See [Churn Definitions & Cohort Methodology](/wiki/churn-methodology) for how these are computed.

| Metric | Mid-Market target | Enterprise target |
|---|---|---|
| GRR | 90% | 96% |
| NRR | 108% | 122% |
| Logo retention | 88% | 95% |
| Average expansion per renewal | +6% | +18% |

Enterprise carries the bulk of NRR because expansion ACV is larger and seat creep is more reliable. Mid-Market retention is structurally lower because the segment is more price-sensitive, more likely to consolidate vendors during cost reviews, and more likely to churn after a price change.

## CS Coverage Model

CSMs are allocated by ARR, not by account count.

| Segment | CSM book size | Touch model |
|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | $4M–$6M ARR per CSM | Pooled, tech-touch + scheduled QBR |
| Enterprise | $1.5M–$2.5M ARR per CSM | Named, monthly cadence, quarterly EBR |

A handful of "Strategic" Enterprise accounts (those over $300K ACV, currently around 12 accounts) have a dedicated CSM at a book size below $1.5M. See [Customer Success Playbook](/wiki/customer-success-playbook) for the cadence detail.

## When Segment Matters in Analysis

Almost every metric in our warehouse should be sliced by segment. A revenue trend that looks neutral at the company level can be masking a Mid-Market problem offset by Enterprise expansion, or the reverse. As a rule of thumb: if a number is moving by more than 5% at the company level, the segment cut is the first thing to pull.

wikiPages.title: Product Portfolio
wikiPages.body:
# Product Portfolio

Helix Cloud sells three products: **Helix Core**, **Helix Alerts**, and **Helix Lineage**. Core is the foundation that every paying account buys. Alerts and Lineage are attach motions sold either with the initial Core contract (land-and-expand) or as expansion on an existing account. This page is the canonical reference for what each product does, how it is priced, and what the attach goals look like.

## Portfolio at a Glance

| Product | Role | GA Date | Q2 2026 Attach Rate | Attach Target |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Helix Core** | Foundation: pipeline observability | 2019-03-01 | 100% (always sold) | n/a |
| **Helix Alerts** | Anomaly detection + alerting | 2021-09-15 | 62% | 70% |
| **Helix Lineage** | Column-level lineage graph | 2025-10-14 | 18% | 35% |

The Lineage attach gap is one of the load-bearing facts for Q2 2026. See [Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo](/wiki/q2-2026-target-and-actuals).

## Helix Core

Helix Core is the foundation product and the reason most customers buy from us. It instruments data pipelines and warehouses to give engineers visibility into job health, data freshness, and pipeline performance.

**Capabilities**

- **Pipeline logs**, structured log capture from Airflow, Dagster, Prefect, and dbt runs
- **Traces**, distributed traces across pipeline DAGs, including upstream dependency timing
- **Metrics**, duration, throughput, row counts, and custom metrics per task
- **Job health monitoring**, pass/fail tracking with rolling baselines
- **Warehouse integration**, Snowflake, BigQuery, Databricks, Redshift query observability
- **Slack and PagerDuty integrations**
- **30-day metric retention** standard; 90 days on higher tiers

**Pricing tiers**

| Tier | Annual list (Mid-Market) | Includes |
|---|---|---|
| Starter | $30,000 | 5 users, 50 monitored pipelines, 30-day retention |
| Growth | $48,000 | 15 users, 200 pipelines, 60-day retention |
| Scale | $78,000 | 40 users, 500 pipelines, 90-day retention, RBAC |

Enterprise pricing is custom and starts at $80K, going up to $500K for very large data orgs.

## Helix Alerts

Helix Alerts adds anomaly detection on top of Core: machine-learning-based baselines for pipeline duration, row counts, and data freshness, plus the routing and on-call infrastructure to make alerts actionable.

**Capabilities**

- **ML baselines**, automatic baselines for time-series metrics with seasonality detection
- **Static thresholds**, for cases where the rule is well-defined
- **Alert routing**, round-robin, escalation policies, schedules
- **Auto-suppress**, known-issue grouping to reduce alert fatigue
- **Slack, PagerDuty, Opsgenie, MS Teams** integrations
- **Alert review dashboards** for weekly tuning

**Pricing**

Helix Alerts is priced as a **percentage uplift on the Core tier**, currently **35%** of list. On a $48K Growth contract, Alerts adds $16,800 ACV. The pricing was designed to make Alerts an easy attach decision; the actual blocker has historically been integration effort, not price.

**Attach motion**

Alerts attach is owned jointly by Sales (at land) and CS (at renewal). The attach rate has been steadily trending up since GA in 2021 and crossed 60% in Q4 2025.

## Helix Lineage

Helix Lineage is our newest product, GA in **October 2025**. It builds a column-level lineage graph across the customer's data stack so engineers can answer questions like "what breaks downstream if I change this column?" or "where does this dashboard's data actually come from?"

**Capabilities**

- **Column-level lineage** parsed from SQL run on the warehouse + dbt manifest
- **Cross-warehouse lineage** for customers running both Snowflake and BigQuery
- **dbt integration**, model graph overlay
- **Impact analysis**, pre-deploy "what breaks" view
- **API access** to the lineage graph for downstream tooling
- **Catalog search**, column descriptions, owners, tags

**Pricing**

Lineage is priced as a **flat add-on** rather than a percentage of Core. The flat fee reflects the fact that lineage value does not scale linearly with Core spend; a small team with complex pipelines can get as much value as a large team with simple ones.

| Segment | Lineage flat fee (annual) |
|---|---|
| Mid-Market | $18,000 |
| Enterprise | $48,000 |

**Attach motion**

Lineage was originally sold by Sales at land and expansion. In Q2 2026, attach came in at **18% against a 35% target**. The product team (under Alex Okafor) believes the gap is partly a motion problem (Sales does not have a strong demo flow for Lineage) and partly an integration friction problem (Lineage requires query history access in the warehouse, which can take a quarter to get through customer security review). A CS-led attach motion is under discussion. See [Customer Success Playbook](/wiki/customer-success-playbook).

## Roadmap Adjacencies

The Product team is currently scoping two future motions: a **Helix Catalog** module (sitting on top of Lineage) and a **Helix Cost** module (warehouse spend observability). Neither is contractable today; neither contributes to ARR. Do not include them in any portfolio analysis until further notice from Alex Okafor.

## Reading Product Mix in the Warehouse

Each subscription line in `revenue.subscriptions` is tagged with a `product` column taking values `core`, `alerts`, or `lineage`. To pull attach rate, count the number of accounts with an active line of a given product and divide by the number of accounts with an active Core line. The canonical SQL is in [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions).

wikiPages.title: Sales Motion & Pipeline Stages
wikiPages.body:
# Sales Motion & Pipeline Stages

This page documents the Helix Cloud sales motion: the seven pipeline stages we use in Salesforce, the owner and exit criteria for each, the typical duration, and current cycle-length trends. The seven-stage model is identical across Mid-Market and Enterprise; what differs between segments is the typical time spent in each stage and the level of cross-functional involvement.

## The Seven Stages

| # | Stage | Owner | Exit Criteria | Typical Duration (MM / Ent) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Prospect | SDR | Account meets ICP; SDR confirms intent signal | Days |
| 2 | Qualify | SDR + AE | BANT-light qualification call; AE accepts opportunity | 3–7 days / 1–2 weeks |
| 3 | Discovery | AE | Documented pain, decision-makers, evaluation criteria | 1–2 weeks / 3–4 weeks |
| 4 | Proof of Value | AE + SE | Successful POV against pre-agreed exit criteria | 2–3 weeks / 4–6 weeks |
| 5 | Proposal | AE | Quote sent, scope agreed | 1 week / 1–2 weeks |
| 6 | Negotiation | AE + RevOps | Terms agreed, redlines closed, security/legal reviews complete | 1–2 weeks / 3–6 weeks |
| 7 | Closed Won / Closed Lost | AE | Contract signed (Won) or DQ reason logged (Lost) | n/a |

A typical Mid-Market deal moves through all seven stages in **6–8 weeks** under normal conditions. A typical Enterprise deal takes **14–22 weeks**. The Q2 2026 Mid-Market cycle stretched to **11 weeks** on average, which is documented in [Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo](/wiki/q2-2026-target-and-actuals).

## Stage Definitions

### 1. Prospect

An account in Prospect has been identified as a potential fit (matches ICP firmographics, has a relevant tech stack, shows an intent signal) but has not yet been contacted, or has been contacted without a response that warrants further pursuit. SDRs own outbound sequencing here. Marketing-sourced inbound MQLs land directly in Qualify, not Prospect.

### 2. Qualify

The SDR has a connected discovery-light call. We use a stripped-down BANT, Budget tentative, Authority identified, Need confirmed, Timeline confirmed, that is more about "is this worth an AE's hour" than a hard qualification gate. Once the AE accepts the opportunity, it moves to Discovery.

### 3. Discovery

This is where the deal becomes real. The AE runs one or more discovery sessions to document the pain, identify all decision-makers, agree on evaluation criteria, and confirm a target timeline. For Enterprise, this stage also includes a paper exchange of security expectations. **Exit criterion:** the AE has a written discovery summary and the prospect has agreed to a POV.

### 4. Proof of Value

The customer connects Helix Cloud to a non-production environment and tests against the success criteria defined in Discovery. SE-led, AE owns the relationship. POV duration is one of the most variable parts of our funnel and the place we lose the most time in slow quarters.

### 5. Proposal

The AE sends a formal quote. Pricing comes from our standard list (see [Pricing & Packaging (2026)](/wiki/pricing-and-packaging)) with discounting per the authority matrix in [Customer Segments](/wiki/customer-segments). For Mid-Market this is often a single one-page quote; for Enterprise it is a multi-page proposal with TCO modeling and a security overview.

### 6. Negotiation

Terms, redlines, and any security or legal review live here. For Enterprise, this is typically the longest stage in absolute time. For Mid-Market, this is usually procurement formality if anything.

### 7. Closed Won / Lost

Closed Won triggers the post-sale handoff to CS and the billing event. Closed Lost requires a DQ (disqualification) reason logged in Salesforce; we report the DQ distribution weekly to identify systemic loss patterns.

## Pipeline Coverage

We target **3.0x pipeline coverage minimum** and **3.5x as healthy**, measured as open-pipeline ACV in stages 3–6 divided by remaining-quarter quota. Coverage is reviewed weekly in the forecast call. See [Forecasting & Pacing](/wiki/forecasting-and-pacing) for the dashboard and ownership detail.

For reference, **Q2 2026 pipeline coverage held at 3.4x**, comfortably within the healthy band. This is a useful fact to keep in mind when diagnosing the Q2 miss: the top of the funnel did not collapse.

## Cycle Length Trend

| Quarter | Mid-Market avg cycle | Enterprise avg cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Q3 2025 | 7 weeks | 16 weeks |
| Q4 2025 | 8 weeks | 17 weeks |
| Q1 2026 | 8 weeks | 17 weeks |
| Q2 2026 | **11 weeks** | 18 weeks |

The Mid-Market cycle expansion from Q1 to Q2 is the largest single-quarter movement we have seen since 2024. Possible drivers include the January 2026 price increase changing prospect expectations, longer security reviews, and macro tightening on Mid-Market budgets.

## Stage Hygiene Rules

To keep the funnel reportable, AEs are expected to:

- Move opportunities into Discovery within **5 business days** of acceptance
- Update next-step dates after every customer touch
- Use the standard DQ reason picklist on every Closed Lost
- Update close date and amount before each Friday's forecast cut

RevOps audits stage hygiene monthly. The data team should assume the stage history in `revenue.pipeline` is reliable; if you see something that looks off, ping Sam Iyer in #revops-help before drawing conclusions.

wikiPages.title: Customer Success Playbook
wikiPages.body:
# Customer Success Playbook

The CS organization at Helix Cloud is run by **Jordan Park (VP CS)** and is responsible for everything that happens after the contract is signed: onboarding, adoption, expansion, renewal, and churn save. This page is the canonical reference for our health-score model, QBR cadence, expansion motion, and escalation paths.

## CS Operating Model

CS is segmented the same way Sales is: Mid-Market CSMs run a pooled book with a tech-touch overlay, Enterprise CSMs run named accounts, and a small Strategic team carries the largest twelve accounts. See [Customer Segments](/wiki/customer-segments) for book sizes.

| Function | Owner | Mandate |
|---|---|---|
| Onboarding | Implementation managers | 60-day to first value |
| Adoption | CSMs | Drive weekly active engineers, integration coverage |
| Expansion | CSMs + AEs | Identify, qualify, and convert expansion opportunities |
| Renewals | Renewals desk | 90-day renewal motion, save plays, contract execution |
| Churn save | Renewals desk + Jordan | Last-stop escalation when a churn is imminent |
| Technical CS | Technical CS engineers | Deep integration and ML model tuning help |

## Health Score Model

The Helix Cloud health score is a composite **0–100** number rolled up daily into `customer_success.health_scores`. CSMs read it in their book view; renewals reads it on a 90-day pre-renewal cohort cut.

| Input | Weight | Source | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weekly active engineers (rolling 28-day) | 30% | `product_usage.usage_daily` | Higher = healthier |
| Integration coverage (% of in-scope pipelines instrumented) | 20% | `product_usage.usage_daily` | Higher = healthier |
| Helix Alerts attach (Y/N) | 10% | `revenue.subscriptions` | Yes = healthier |
| Helix Lineage attach (Y/N) | 5% | `revenue.subscriptions` | Yes = healthier |
| Open P1/P2 tickets aged >7 days | 15% | `customer_success.tickets` | Lower = healthier |
| Time since last QBR | 10% | CS CRM | Lower = healthier |
| Executive sponsor identified | 10% | CS CRM | Yes = healthier |

Scores are bucketed for reporting:

- **Green** (≥75): on track, expansion candidate
- **Yellow** (50–74): watch, monthly check-in
- **Red** (<50): risk, weekly action plan + manager involvement

A health score on its own does not trigger churn. It triggers a workflow that, executed well, prevents churn. CSMs are accountable for the workflow, not the score.

## QBR Cadence

| Segment | Cadence | Format | Attendees |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | Quarterly (light) | 30-min Zoom, standard deck | CSM + Head of Data |
| Enterprise | Quarterly | 60-min Zoom, custom deck | CSM + AE + customer exec sponsor |
| Strategic Enterprise | Quarterly EBR + monthly touch | In-person where possible | CSM + AE + Jordan or Maya + customer VP |

Every QBR ends with a documented action plan and an explicit expansion qualifier ("is there a path to growing this account in the next two quarters, yes/no/maybe, and why").

## Expansion Motion

Expansion is owned jointly by CS and Sales. The CSM identifies and qualifies, the AE quotes and closes. The current expansion mix:

| Expansion type | Share of expansion ARR (Q1 2026) |
|---|---|
| Seat increase (Core) | ~45% |
| Tier upgrade (Core) | ~20% |
| Helix Alerts attach | ~20% |
| Helix Lineage attach | ~15% |

The Lineage attach number was forecast to grow to ~30% of expansion in Q2 2026 and did not, see [Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo](/wiki/q2-2026-target-and-actuals). The product team and Jordan's team are scoping a **CS-led Lineage motion** for H2 2026.

## Renewal Motion

Renewals are owned by a dedicated **renewals desk** that reports to Jordan. The standard motion starts **120 days before renewal date** and runs through five gates:

1. **T-120: Health check.** CSM and renewals lead review health score, usage trend, and any open tickets.
2. **T-90: Renewal conversation.** First explicit conversation with the customer's economic buyer.
3. **T-60: Quote sent.** Includes any expansion or contraction discussion.
4. **T-30: Verbal commit.** Either commit to renew, commit to churn, or escalation.
5. **T-7: Paper signed** or churn play executed.

The motion runs to completion on every account, including Mid-Market. The 2026-01-15 price increase added a sixth checkpoint at **T-150** specifically for accounts hitting the new pricing: a price-impact preview and a grandfathering eligibility review. That checkpoint is meant to surface churn risk early; whether it has been effective is something the analytics team should look into.

## Churn Save Process

When a renewal is at risk:

1. CSM logs the at-risk flag in the CS CRM with a reason code
2. Renewals desk and CSM build a save plan (concession, scope change, or escalation)
3. If the plan requires discounting beyond CSM authority, RevOps approves
4. If the customer still wants to leave, Jordan personally calls within 48 hours
5. Final disposition is logged in `revenue.events` as either a `contraction` (saved at lower scope) or `churn` (full termination)

## Escalation Paths

| Issue | Route to |
|---|---|
| Product bug blocking adoption | Technical CS → Engineering on-call |
| Pricing or commercial dispute | Renewals desk → RevOps → VP Revenue |
| Strategic exec relationship | CSM → Jordan or Lindsey |
| Data accuracy concern in our reporting | CSM → Sam Iyer (RevOps) |
| Security or trust escalation | CSM → Security on-call (#sec-help) |

## What CS Owns in the Data Warehouse

CS owns `customer_success.health_scores` and `customer_success.tickets`. Both tables are documented in [Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)](/wiki/data-warehouse-schema). Health score components are recalculated daily at 06:00 UTC; if a score looks stale, check the daily DAG status in #revops-help before drawing conclusions.

wikiPages.title: Pricing & Packaging (2026)
wikiPages.body:
# Pricing & Packaging (2026)

This page documents Helix Cloud's official 2026 list pricing for **Helix Core**, **Helix Alerts**, and **Helix Lineage**, the discounting authority matrix, and the **2026-01-15 Mid-Market price increase**. It supersedes all prior pricing pages. Quotes generated after the effective date use the prices on this page.

## Helix Core, Mid-Market List Pricing (effective 2026-01-15)

Helix Core is sold in three Mid-Market tiers. Tiers are differentiated by user seats, monitored pipeline count, and metric retention.

| Tier | Annual list | Users | Pipelines | Retention | RBAC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Starter | $30,000 | 5 | 50 | 30 days | No |
| Growth | $48,000 | 15 | 200 | 60 days | No |
| Scale | $78,000 | 40 | 500 | 90 days | Yes |

Mid-Market tiers above Scale roll automatically into Enterprise list at $80K+.

## Helix Core, Enterprise List Pricing

Enterprise pricing is custom. We anchor on three reference points and negotiate from there.

| Reference profile | Anchor list | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Enterprise | $120,000 | 75 users, 1,000 pipelines, 90-day retention, SSO/RBAC |
| Strategic Enterprise | $250,000 | Custom user count, 2,500 pipelines, dedicated CSM |
| Platinum Enterprise | $400,000+ | Unlimited users, dedicated infrastructure, named support |

## Helix Alerts, Pricing

Helix Alerts is priced as a **percentage of Core list**, currently **35%**. The intent is to make Alerts a near-default attach.

| Scenario | Core annual | Alerts annual |
|---|---|---|
| Growth tier customer attaches Alerts | $48,000 | $16,800 |
| Standard Enterprise attaches Alerts | $120,000 | $42,000 |

## Helix Lineage, Pricing

Helix Lineage is priced as a **flat annual add-on**, not a percentage. The flat fee reflects the fact that lineage value does not scale linearly with Core spend.

| Segment | Lineage flat fee |
|---|---|
| Mid-Market | $18,000 |
| Enterprise | $48,000 |

See [Product Portfolio](/wiki/product-portfolio) for product capability detail.

## 2026-01-15 Mid-Market Price Increase

**On 2026-01-15, Helix Cloud raised Mid-Market list prices on Helix Core by 18%.** The increase was approved by the executive team in November 2025 and announced internally on 2025-12-01.

**Rationale (as documented in the pricing-change memo)**

- List pricing had not been adjusted since 2022
- Cost-to-serve had grown materially as customers ran more pipelines per account
- Benchmarking against comparable B2B observability tools showed our list was below market
- Mid-Market gross margins were running below the company target by ~6 percentage points

**Magnitude**

The 18% list increase applied to all three Mid-Market tiers:

| Tier | Pre-2026-01-15 list | Post-2026-01-15 list | Delta |
|---|---|---|---|
| Starter | $25,400 | $30,000 | +$4,600 |
| Growth | $40,700 | $48,000 | +$7,300 |
| Scale | $66,100 | $78,000 | +$11,900 |

**Grandfathering policy**

Existing Mid-Market customers were grandfathered on their then-current contract. The new list price applies at their next renewal. Specifically:

- A customer renewing on 2026-02-10 gets the new price at that renewal
- A customer on a 12-month contract that ends 2026-09-30 stays at their old rate until 2026-09-30
- Mid-contract upsells default to a blended rate; the renewals desk can apply old or new list on a case-by-case basis with RevOps sign-off

**Communication**

Customers were notified via two emails:
1. A general announcement to all Mid-Market customers on 2025-12-15 with the headline change
2. A personalized renewal-impact email **120 days before each customer's renewal date** with the customer's specific dollar change

CS was given a talking-points doc and a discounting flexibility envelope (up to 8% off list at renewal without RevOps approval; up to 15% with approval).

**Enterprise was not affected by this change.** Enterprise contracts are individually negotiated and do not reference Mid-Market list.

## Discounting Authority

| Tier of discount | Mid-Market approver | Enterprise approver |
|---|---|---|
| 0–10% off list | AE | AE |
| 10–15% off list | AE Manager | AE |
| 15–25% off list | RevOps | AE Manager |
| 25%+ off list | VP Revenue | VP Revenue |
| Non-standard terms (multi-year prepay, custom SLA) | RevOps + Legal | RevOps + Legal |

Discounting is logged in the opportunity record. RevOps audits monthly and surfaces any over-discounting in the weekly forecast call.

## Renewals Pricing

| Scenario | Default behavior |
|---|---|
| Mid-Market same-scope renewal (pre-2026 cohort) | Apply new list at renewal per grandfathering policy |
| Mid-Market same-scope renewal (post-2026 cohort) | 5% CPI escalator, waivable |
| Enterprise renewal | Per-contract escalation clause |
| Renewal with expansion | New list, blended where renewals desk discretion applies |
| Renewal with contraction | Old list, no escalator |

## How Pricing Flows into the Warehouse

Each subscription line in `revenue.subscriptions` carries a `list_price_arr` and a `contracted_arr`. The delta is the discount granted on that line. The price increase is observable as a step change in median Mid-Market `list_price_arr` for new contracts after 2026-01-15. See [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions) for a recipe.

wikiPages.title: Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)
wikiPages.body:
# Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)

All Helix Cloud first-party analytics data lives in the **`helix-cloud-prod`** BigQuery project. This page documents every dataset and table the RevOps Analytics team uses day-to-day. Treat it as the canonical column-level reference. For naming, partitioning, and standard query patterns, see [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions).

## Project & Datasets

| Project | Dataset | Owner | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| `helix-cloud-prod` | `revenue` | RevOps | Account, subscription, event, and pipeline data |
| `helix-cloud-prod` | `customer_success` | CS Ops | Daily health scores, support tickets |
| `helix-cloud-prod` | `product_usage` | Product Analytics | Per-account daily usage rollups |

Other datasets exist (Finance's `gaap`, Marketing's `campaigns`, Engineering's `telemetry`) but they are out of scope for revenue-side analysis and not documented here.

## `revenue.accounts`

One row per customer account. The dimension table that almost every revenue query joins to.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `account_id` | STRING | Stable surrogate key (`acc_xxxxx`) |
| `account_name` | STRING | Legal customer name (e.g., "Northwind Logistics") |
| `segment` | STRING | `mid_market` or `enterprise` |
| `employee_count` | INT64 | Last firmographic snapshot |
| `industry` | STRING | High-level NAICS-derived industry |
| `geo` | STRING | `na`, `emea`, or `apac` |
| `account_owner_id` | STRING | AE id, joins to `revenue.users` (not documented here) |
| `csm_id` | STRING | CSM id |
| `first_contract_date` | DATE | Date of first ever new-logo event |
| `current_arr` | NUMERIC | Sum of active subscription line ACVs |
| `status` | STRING | `active`, `churned`, `inactive_free` |
| `created_at` | TIMESTAMP | Row creation timestamp |
| `updated_at` | TIMESTAMP | Last update timestamp |

## `revenue.subscriptions`

One row per active or historically-active subscription line. Each line is tied to one product. A customer with Core, Alerts, and Lineage has three rows.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `subscription_id` | STRING | Stable surrogate key |
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts.account_id` |
| `product` | STRING | `core`, `alerts`, or `lineage` |
| `tier` | STRING | For Core: `starter`, `growth`, `scale`, or `enterprise`. Null for Alerts/Lineage. |
| `seats` | INT64 | Contracted user seats (Core only) |
| `contract_start_date` | DATE | First day of this contract term |
| `contract_end_date` | DATE | Last day of this contract term |
| `list_price_arr` | NUMERIC | Annualized list price before discount |
| `contracted_arr` | NUMERIC | Annualized contracted ARR (the metric that rolls to ARR) |
| `discount_pct` | NUMERIC | (`list_price_arr` – `contracted_arr`) / `list_price_arr` |
| `auto_renew` | BOOL | True if the contract auto-renews |
| `status` | STRING | `active`, `expired`, `cancelled` |
| `created_at` | TIMESTAMP | |
| `updated_at` | TIMESTAMP | |

## `revenue.events`

One row per ARR-changing event. This is the spine of every retention and net-new-ARR analysis. Every event reconciles to a change in `revenue.subscriptions`.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `event_id` | STRING | Stable surrogate key |
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts.account_id` |
| `subscription_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.subscriptions.subscription_id` |
| `event_type` | STRING | One of: `new`, `expansion`, `contraction`, `churn`, `reactivation` |
| `event_date` | DATE | Effective date of the change |
| `event_amount_arr` | NUMERIC | Signed delta to ARR (positive for new/expansion/reactivation, negative for contraction/churn) |
| `reason_code` | STRING | Categorical reason (e.g., `price_increase`, `seat_decrease`, `tier_downgrade`, `competitor_loss`, `consolidation`) |
| `notes` | STRING | Free text from the CSM or renewals desk |
| `recorded_by` | STRING | User id of the person who logged the event |
| `created_at` | TIMESTAMP | |

`reason_code` is **not always populated** for events before Q4 2024. Anything from 2025 forward should have it.

## `revenue.pipeline`

Daily snapshot of Salesforce opportunities. Used for pipeline coverage, stage-conversion, and cycle-length analysis.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `snapshot_date` | DATE | The date this snapshot row represents |
| `opportunity_id` | STRING | Salesforce opportunity id |
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts.account_id` (null for net-new prospects) |
| `account_name` | STRING | Display name on opp |
| `segment` | STRING | `mid_market` or `enterprise` |
| `stage` | STRING | `prospect`, `qualify`, `discovery`, `pov`, `proposal`, `negotiation`, `closed_won`, `closed_lost` |
| `amount_acv` | NUMERIC | Forecasted ACV |
| `forecast_category` | STRING | `pipeline`, `best_case`, `commit`, `closed` |
| `close_date` | DATE | Expected close date |
| `created_at_opp` | DATE | Opportunity creation date |
| `days_in_stage` | INT64 | Days since last stage change |
| `owner_id` | STRING | AE id |
| `dq_reason` | STRING | DQ reason if `closed_lost` |

## `customer_success.health_scores`

One row per account per day. Health score recomputed daily at 06:00 UTC.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts` |
| `score_date` | DATE | Date of the rollup |
| `score` | INT64 | 0–100 composite |
| `bucket` | STRING | `green`, `yellow`, `red` |
| `weekly_active_engineers` | INT64 | 28-day rolling |
| `integration_coverage_pct` | NUMERIC | % of in-scope pipelines instrumented |
| `open_p1_p2_tickets_7d` | INT64 | Open P1/P2 tickets aged > 7 days |
| `days_since_last_qbr` | INT64 | |
| `has_exec_sponsor` | BOOL | |

## `customer_success.tickets`

One row per support ticket.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `ticket_id` | STRING | Stable surrogate key |
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts` |
| `created_at` | TIMESTAMP | |
| `resolved_at` | TIMESTAMP | Null if still open |
| `severity` | STRING | `p1`, `p2`, `p3`, `p4` |
| `category` | STRING | `bug`, `question`, `feature_request`, `integration` |
| `product` | STRING | `core`, `alerts`, `lineage`, `platform` |
| `assignee_id` | STRING | |
| `status` | STRING | `open`, `pending`, `resolved`, `closed` |

## `product_usage.usage_daily`

One row per account per day. Rolled up from raw engineering telemetry.

| Column | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `account_id` | STRING | FK to `revenue.accounts` |
| `usage_date` | DATE | |
| `active_engineers` | INT64 | Unique engineers who took an action that day |
| `pipelines_observed` | INT64 | Distinct monitored pipeline count |
| `events_ingested` | INT64 | Total telemetry events ingested for this account |
| `alerts_fired` | INT64 | Count of alerts fired (requires Helix Alerts) |
| `lineage_queries` | INT64 | Lineage API call count (requires Helix Lineage) |

## Sync Cadence

- `revenue.*` tables: continuous CDC from the billing system, partitions finalized at 02:00 UTC daily
- `customer_success.health_scores`: full recompute at 06:00 UTC
- `customer_success.tickets`: continuous CDC from Zendesk
- `product_usage.usage_daily`: rollup at 04:00 UTC daily

If you are looking at "today's" data before 06:30 UTC, treat health scores as one day stale. Otherwise everything is current.

wikiPages.title: BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries
wikiPages.body:
# BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries

This page documents the conventions used in the `helix-cloud-prod` BigQuery project, the time-zone handling we use for board reporting, and a set of authoritative SQL recipes you should copy into new analyses instead of rewriting from scratch. Pair this with [Data Warehouse Schema (BigQuery)](/wiki/data-warehouse-schema) for column-level reference.

## Naming Conventions

| Object | Convention |
|---|---|
| Project | `helix-cloud-prod` (production), `helix-cloud-dev` (sandbox) |
| Dataset | lowercase, snake_case, single-word domain (`revenue`, `customer_success`, `product_usage`) |
| Tables | lowercase, snake_case, singular for dimensions (`accounts`) and singular event noun (`events`) or `_daily` for daily fact rollups |
| Columns | lowercase, snake_case |
| Booleans | `is_*` or `has_*` prefix |
| Dates | `*_date` (DATE) vs `*_at` (TIMESTAMP) |
| Money | `*_arr`, `*_acv`, `*_amount` in NUMERIC, always in USD |
| Identifiers | surrogate keys `xxx_id`, never natural keys |

Anything that breaks these conventions is either a legacy import or a mistake. If in doubt, check with Sam Iyer.

## Partition & Cluster Strategy

| Table | Partition | Cluster |
|---|---|---|
| `revenue.events` | `event_date` (daily) | `account_id`, `event_type` |
| `revenue.pipeline` | `snapshot_date` (daily) | `account_id`, `stage` |
| `customer_success.health_scores` | `score_date` (daily) | `account_id` |
| `customer_success.tickets` | DATE(`created_at`) | `account_id`, `severity` |
| `product_usage.usage_daily` | `usage_date` (daily) | `account_id` |
| `revenue.accounts` | unpartitioned | `segment` |
| `revenue.subscriptions` | unpartitioned | `account_id` |

**Always filter on the partition key** in event/snapshot tables. Forgetting the partition predicate on `revenue.events` is the easiest way to scan terabytes for no reason and is the most common reason a new analyst's query gets killed.

## Time Zones

We have **two canonical time zones**:

- **UTC**, used for everything in the warehouse. All `*_at` timestamps are UTC. All partition dates are UTC.
- **America/Los_Angeles (Pacific)**, used for the **board readout** and for the weekly Monday metrics email. Pacific is the only customer-facing reporting time zone.

When converting for board reporting, use `DATETIME(t, 'America/Los_Angeles')`. Quarter boundaries for board reporting are also Pacific. The day a Pacific quarter ends is **one hour later in UTC**, which matters for late-arriving events on the last day of the quarter, pull them with a Pacific filter and a UTC window large enough to include them.

**Do not mix time zones in a single query.** Convert once at the boundary; aggregate in one zone.

## Fiscal Calendar

Helix Cloud operates on a **calendar fiscal year**. FY2026 = January 1, 2026 through December 31, 2026.

| Quarter | Start (Pacific) | End (Pacific) |
|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | 2026-01-01 | 2026-03-31 |
| Q2 2026 | 2026-04-01 | 2026-06-30 |
| Q3 2026 | 2026-07-01 | 2026-09-30 |
| Q4 2026 | 2026-10-01 | 2026-12-31 |

## Standard Query Recipes

The three recipes below are the most-used patterns on the RevOps Analytics team. Copy them as-is, modify the date filter, and run.

### Recipe 1: Quarterly net new ARR by segment

```sql
WITH evt AS (
  SELECT
    a.segment,
    e.event_type,
    SUM(e.event_amount_arr) AS amt
  FROM `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.events` e
  JOIN `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.accounts` a
    USING (account_id)
  WHERE e.event_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-04-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
  GROUP BY 1, 2
)
SELECT
  segment,
  SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'new' THEN amt END)         AS new_logo,
  SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'expansion' THEN amt END)   AS expansion,
  SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'reactivation' THEN amt END) AS reactivation,
  SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'contraction' THEN amt END) AS contraction,
  SUM(CASE WHEN event_type = 'churn' THEN amt END)       AS churn,
  SUM(amt) AS net_new_arr
FROM evt
GROUP BY segment
ORDER BY segment;
```

### Recipe 2: Quarterly gross churn rate by segment

```sql
WITH beginning AS (
  SELECT
    a.segment,
    SUM(s.contracted_arr) AS bop_arr
  FROM `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.subscriptions` s
  JOIN `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.accounts` a
    USING (account_id)
  WHERE s.contract_start_date < DATE '2026-04-01'
    AND (s.contract_end_date IS NULL OR s.contract_end_date >= DATE '2026-04-01')
  GROUP BY segment
),
churned AS (
  SELECT
    a.segment,
    SUM(ABS(e.event_amount_arr)) AS churn_arr
  FROM `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.events` e
  JOIN `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.accounts` a
    USING (account_id)
  WHERE e.event_type = 'churn'
    AND e.event_date BETWEEN DATE '2026-04-01' AND DATE '2026-06-30'
  GROUP BY segment
)
SELECT
  b.segment,
  b.bop_arr,
  COALESCE(c.churn_arr, 0) AS churn_arr,
  SAFE_DIVIDE(COALESCE(c.churn_arr, 0), b.bop_arr) AS gross_churn_rate
FROM beginning b
LEFT JOIN churned c USING (segment)
ORDER BY segment;
```

### Recipe 3: Helix Lineage attach rate

```sql
WITH active_accounts AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT account_id
  FROM `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.subscriptions`
  WHERE product = 'core'
    AND status = 'active'
),
lineage_accounts AS (
  SELECT DISTINCT account_id
  FROM `helix-cloud-prod.revenue.subscriptions`
  WHERE product = 'lineage'
    AND status = 'active'
)
SELECT
  COUNT(DISTINCT a.account_id) AS core_customers,
  COUNT(DISTINCT l.account_id) AS lineage_customers,
  SAFE_DIVIDE(COUNT(DISTINCT l.account_id), COUNT(DISTINCT a.account_id)) AS attach_rate
FROM active_accounts a
LEFT JOIN lineage_accounts l USING (account_id);
```

## Performance Hints

- **Always filter on the partition key.** Saves dollars and minutes.
- **Use `SAFE_DIVIDE` for ratios.** Avoids divide-by-zero blowups.
- **Cluster fields go in `WHERE` and `JOIN` early.** BigQuery is smart but not magic.
- **Materialize intermediate CTEs only if the same logic appears 3+ times.** Otherwise CTEs are fine.

## Where to Get Help

Slack `#revops-help` is the primary channel. Sam Iyer is on for warehouse questions; the broader RevOps team handles forecast and pipeline questions. For SQL pairing, post the query and the expected output and someone will jump on.

wikiPages.title: Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo
wikiPages.body:
# Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo

This memo documents the Q2 2026 net new ARR plan, the components that built up to it, and the Q2 actuals as of close (June 30, 2026). It is the source of truth for the numbers feeding the **2026-07-15 board readout**. Diagnosis of variance is **not** in this memo; diagnosis is owned by the RevOps Analytics team and will be appended once complete.

## Headline

| Measure | Plan | Actual | Variance | Variance % |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Net New ARR | $9.5M | $7.6M | -$1.9M | -20.0% |
| Ending ARR | $67.7M | $65.8M | -$1.9M | -2.8% |

Q2 2026 net new ARR came in **$1.9M (-20.0%) below plan**. Ending ARR of **$65.8M** carries an equivalent shortfall against the implied target. Pipeline coverage and pacing dashboards did not flag this gap until the final 30 days of the quarter, which is itself a topic for the analytics review.

## Board Commitment

The $9.5M Q2 net new ARR figure was committed to the board at the **2026-02-12 board meeting** as part of the FY2026 plan refresh. The commitment was based on:

- Q1 2026 actual net new ARR of $8.4M (slight outperformance vs plan)
- The H1 2026 pipeline coverage forecast
- Assumed continuation of Q1 Mid-Market churn trends (then 6.8% gross)
- The Helix Lineage attach motion ramping to a 35% attach target through Q2
- No incremental headwinds modeled from the 2026-01-15 Mid-Market price increase

The plan was approved unanimously. A copy of the plan deck is in the executive shared drive under `FY2026 Plan / Board Readouts / 2026-02-12`.

## Components Plan (set in February)

The $9.5M target was built up from five components per our standard methodology (see [Revenue Model & Definitions](/wiki/revenue-model-and-definitions)).

| Component | Q2 Plan |
|---|---|
| New-Logo ARR | $6.8M |
| Expansion ARR | $4.9M |
| Reactivation ARR | $0.1M |
| Contraction ARR | -$0.6M |
| Gross Churn ARR | -$1.7M |
| **Net New ARR** | **$9.5M** |

The plan assumed normal-quarter seasonality, no major customer-specific events, and the Lineage attach motion delivering ~$0.9M of the expansion line.

## Q2 Actuals (set in July)

The actual five-component breakdown is shown below alongside the plan.

| Component | Q2 Plan | Q2 Actual | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| New-Logo ARR | $6.8M | $6.2M | -$0.6M |
| Expansion ARR | $4.9M | $4.0M | -$0.9M |
| Reactivation ARR | $0.1M | $0.1M | $0.0M |
| Contraction ARR | -$0.6M | -$1.1M | -$0.5M |
| Gross Churn ARR | -$1.7M | -$1.6M | +$0.1M |
| **Net New ARR** | **$9.5M** | **$7.6M** | **-$1.9M** |

Note that **gross churn ARR came in slightly favorable in aggregate dollars** versus plan. Aggregate dollars can hide segment-level dynamics; the rolling churn rate by segment is documented separately in [Churn Definitions & Cohort Methodology](/wiki/churn-methodology).

## Segment View

| Segment | Q2 Plan Net New ARR | Q2 Actual Net New ARR | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | $3.6M | $2.4M | -$1.2M |
| Enterprise | $5.9M | $5.2M | -$0.7M |

The Mid-Market shortfall is concentrated; the Enterprise shortfall is dispersed across new-logo softness, expansion light, and three contract restructures.

## Three Enterprise Restructures

Three Enterprise accounts restructured to lower tiers during Q2. None churned; all remain customers.

| Account | Pre-restructure ACV | Post-restructure ACV | Delta | Reason (recorded) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Northwind Logistics | $220K | $140K | -$80K | Scope reduction |
| Verity Insurance | $185K | $125K | -$60K | Consolidation |
| Atlas Manufacturing | $150K | $100K | -$50K | Procurement-led re-negotiation |

These three together contribute roughly $190K of the contraction line. Reason codes were logged by the renewals desk per the standard process in [Customer Success Playbook](/wiki/customer-success-playbook).

## Pipeline & Pacing Snapshot

For context, the pacing dashboard recorded the following pipeline coverage at the start of each Q2 month:

| Date | Pipeline coverage |
|---|---|
| 2026-04-01 | 3.5x |
| 2026-05-01 | 3.5x |
| 2026-06-01 | 3.4x |

Coverage held within the healthy band throughout the quarter (target ≥ 3.0x, healthy ≥ 3.5x). See [Forecasting & Pacing](/wiki/forecasting-and-pacing) for how this dashboard works and who owns each forecast number.

## Engineering Incident Log (Q2 2026)

For completeness, Q2 saw **one P1 platform incident**: a 41-minute outage on **2026-05-08** affecting the alert delivery pipeline in the US-East region. No customer revenue impact was recorded; SLA credit was issued to two customers per the standard credit policy.

## Cycle Length

Average Mid-Market sales cycle stretched from 8 weeks (Q1) to **11 weeks (Q2)**, the largest single-quarter movement in our recent history. Enterprise cycle moved from 17 to 18 weeks (within normal variance).

## Next Steps

The RevOps Analytics team has been asked to produce a definitive diagnosis of the variance ahead of the **2026-07-15 board readout**. The diagnosis should reconcile:

1. The $1.9M aggregate gap
2. The Mid-Market vs Enterprise split
3. The five-component variance above
4. Recommendations for H2 2026

The diagnosis is a separate document; this memo is the data baseline it operates on.

wikiPages.title: Churn Definitions & Cohort Methodology
wikiPages.body:
# Churn Definitions & Cohort Methodology

This page is the canonical reference for how Helix Cloud defines and measures churn. It exists so that any analyst, board member, or new CSM can answer the question "what does our churn rate actually mean" without ambiguity. Definitions here override anything stated less formally in dashboards or decks.

## Two Senses of "Churn"

The word "churn" gets used in two senses internally. Both are correct; the difference matters.

| Term | Counts which events | Sign |
|---|---|---|
| **Gross churn** | Full termination only (`event_type = 'churn'`) | Always positive (loss) |
| **Net churn** | Gross churn + contraction, net of expansion | Can be positive or negative |

Logo churn (counting accounts, not ARR) is also reported separately as a sanity check.

## Definitions

### Gross Churn ARR

Sum of `event_amount_arr` (absolute value) for all rows where `event_type = 'churn'` in the period.

### Gross Churn Rate

```
Gross Churn Rate = Gross Churn ARR (period) / Beginning ARR (period)
```

Beginning ARR is computed as the sum of `contracted_arr` across all subscription lines whose `contract_start_date < period_start` and (`contract_end_date IS NULL` OR `contract_end_date >= period_start`).

### Net Churn ARR

```
Net Churn ARR = Gross Churn ARR + Contraction ARR – Expansion ARR
```

Note that Net Churn ARR can be **negative**, which means expansion outpaced churn and contraction. A negative net churn is a healthy SaaS signal.

### Net Churn Rate

```
Net Churn Rate = Net Churn ARR / Beginning ARR
```

### Gross Revenue Retention (GRR)

```
GRR = 1 – Gross Churn Rate
```

GRR is reported on a **rolling four-quarter** basis for any external or board context, in-quarter for pacing only.

### Net Revenue Retention (NRR)

```
NRR = (Beginning ARR – Gross Churn ARR – Contraction ARR + Expansion ARR) / Beginning ARR
```

NRR is also reported on a rolling four-quarter basis externally.

## Cohort Methodology

Helix Cloud uses **renewal-cohort** churn measurement: an account is considered "up for churn" in the quarter its contract renewal occurs. This is the methodology that aligns to how the renewals desk works, the team is responsible for the cohort of accounts renewing in the quarter, not for ARR sitting on multi-year deals that are not in the renewal window.

### Cohort Construction

1. For the period, identify all `revenue.subscriptions` rows whose `contract_end_date` falls within the period and whose initial product was `core`. These are the renewal cohort.
2. Look up whether each cohort account has a follow-on `revenue.subscriptions` row for Core starting within 60 days of the original `contract_end_date`.
3. If yes, the account renewed (and any contraction or expansion is captured in that follow-on line).
4. If no, the account churned; we expect a corresponding `event_type = 'churn'` row in `revenue.events` confirming the same.
5. Segment is taken from `revenue.accounts.segment` as of the renewal date.

This methodology is what we use in the board deck. The "raw" alternative, count every `event_type = 'churn'` divided by beginning ARR, gives a similar but not identical number; both are reported, and we prefer the cohort number for board-level external reporting.

## Rolling-Quarter Methodology

For GRR/NRR, we use a **rolling four-quarter window** ending at the quarter being reported. Specifically:

- Beginning ARR is taken at the start of the rolling window (15 months ago, since we want the four quarters that ended in the reporting quarter)
- Sum the gross churn ARR, contraction ARR, and expansion ARR over those four quarters
- Apply the GRR / NRR formula

This is the standard SaaS practice and smooths the noise that single-quarter retention numbers can carry.

## Current Values (Q2 2026)

The table below is informational. It is regenerated at the end of every quarter and frozen at board readout. These are the numbers being prepared for the **2026-07-15 board readout**.

### In-quarter gross churn rate by segment

| Segment | Q1 2026 gross churn rate | Q2 2026 gross churn rate | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | 6.8% | **11.2%** | +4.4 pts |
| Enterprise | 2.1% | **2.4%** | +0.3 pts |

The Mid-Market jump is the largest quarter-over-quarter change in the segment's history. It is the line item the RevOps Analytics team has been asked to explain by 2026-07-15.

### Rolling four-quarter GRR/NRR by segment

| Segment | Rolling GRR (through Q2 2026) | Rolling NRR (through Q2 2026) |
|---|---|---|
| Mid-Market | 91.2% | 104.1% |
| Enterprise | 97.0% | 119.8% |
| Blended | 94.6% | 113.6% |

Targets for FY2026 were Mid-Market GRR 90% / NRR 108% and Enterprise GRR 96% / NRR 122%. Both segments are tracking below NRR target; Enterprise is at GRR target, Mid-Market is at GRR target but trending the wrong way.

## What This Methodology Catches (and Misses)

The cohort methodology is precise about renewal-quarter behavior but slow: a Mid-Market price increase that took effect on 2026-01-15 only shows up in this methodology as accounts hit their renewals, generally one quarter later. That is why a Q1 list price change can produce a Q2 churn signal.

For something faster, use the in-quarter "raw" churn rate (count of `event_type = 'churn'` ARR over the period's beginning ARR). The two numbers should converge over a full year.

## Where to Pull This in BigQuery

The canonical SQL for in-quarter gross churn rate is in [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions). For rolling-quarter cuts, expand the date filter and union four single-quarter results. There is a wrapper view planned but not yet shipped; for now, write the SQL.

wikiPages.title: Forecasting & Pacing
wikiPages.body:
# Forecasting & Pacing

Forecasting at Helix Cloud is a weekly motion that culminates in a Friday number to the executive team. This page documents how the weekly forecast call works, the pipeline coverage targets we operate to, the pacing dashboard, and which leader owns which forecast number. It also flags the limits of the methodology, which are useful to keep in mind when interpreting in-quarter pacing data.

## The Weekly Forecast Cadence

The forecast week runs Monday to Friday on a fixed cadence.

| Day | What happens |
|---|---|
| Monday | RevOps publishes Monday metrics email and refreshes pacing dashboard with weekend data |
| Tuesday | AEs review their pipeline; close-date hygiene push |
| Wednesday | Sales managers run team forecast calls; numbers roll up |
| Thursday | Maya runs the cross-segment forecast call with all sales managers + Jordan + Sam |
| Friday morning | Cut. RevOps locks the forecast and publishes the weekly forecast email to executives |

Anything that moves after Friday cut goes into the next week's forecast.

## Forecast Categories

We use four forecast categories on every open opportunity in `revenue.pipeline`. The AE sets the category; the manager re-categorizes during the Wednesday call.

| Category | Definition | Roll-up behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `closed` | Already closed-won in the quarter | Always included |
| `commit` | AE is committed to close in the quarter (high-confidence) | Always included |
| `best_case` | Realistic but not committed | Included only in "best case" sum |
| `pipeline` | Open opportunity but not forecast to close | Excluded from forecast sums |

The weekly forecast number to executives is **closed + commit**, with **best case** reported as the upside number.

## Pipeline Coverage Targets

| Target | Threshold | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum coverage | 3.0x | Below this, escalation to VP Revenue |
| Healthy coverage | 3.5x | Operating range |
| Strong coverage | 4.0x+ | Often a sign of slow conversion, worth a look |

Coverage is computed as:

```
Coverage = (sum of ACV in stages 3–6, open opps, with close date in the quarter)
         / (quota remaining for the quarter)
```

Mid-Market and Enterprise are tracked separately; the company-level number is informational. For Q2 2026, **company-level coverage held at 3.4x throughout**, well within the healthy band. Coverage was not the issue this quarter.

## The Pacing Dashboard

The pacing dashboard is a Looker-on-BigQuery view that RevOps owns. It shows, for each week of the quarter:

| Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| Closed-won ARR cumulative | Sum of net new ARR booked to date in the quarter |
| Implied weekly run-rate | Closed-won cumulative / weeks elapsed |
| Linear-pacing target | (quarter target) × (weeks elapsed / 13) |
| Pacing variance | Closed-won cumulative − linear-pacing target |
| Pipeline coverage | (see above) |
| Forecast (commit) | Sum of closed + commit |
| Forecast (best case) | Sum of closed + commit + best case |

The dashboard is refreshed each morning from the `revenue.pipeline` and `revenue.events` tables, with snapshot semantics so that a comparison week-over-week is honest. Sam Iyer owns the underlying model; if you see a number that does not match what you computed by hand, post in #revops-help.

## What the Pacing Dashboard Caught (and Missed) in Q2 2026

For context, here is what the dashboard looked like during Q2 2026:

| Week | Pacing variance | Pipeline coverage | Forecast (commit) |
|---|---|---|---|
| W1 | +$0.0M | 3.5x | $9.5M |
| W4 | -$0.2M | 3.5x | $9.4M |
| W8 | -$0.5M | 3.5x | $9.2M |
| W11 | -$0.9M | 3.4x | $8.7M |
| W13 | -$1.9M | 3.4x | $7.6M |

The gap to plan only opened materially in the last four weeks. Coverage stayed strong throughout. The pacing dashboard correctly tracked the variance once it appeared but did not have a leading indicator that anticipated the Mid-Market renewal-cohort churn that materialized late in the quarter. This is a methodology gap that the RevOps Analytics team is expected to flag in any diagnosis.

## Forecast Ownership

Each piece of the quarterly forecast has a named owner. The owner is the person whose name goes on the number in the weekly executive email.

| Number | Owner |
|---|---|
| Total net new ARR forecast | Maya Chen (VP Revenue) |
| New-logo ARR | AE managers (Mid-Market and Enterprise), rolled up by Maya |
| Expansion ARR | Jordan Park (VP CS), in partnership with sales managers |
| Contraction & churn ARR | Jordan Park, via the renewals desk lead |
| Pipeline coverage | Sam Iyer (RevOps) reports the number; sales managers own the underlying pipeline |
| Pacing dashboard accuracy | Sam Iyer |
| Lineage attach forecast | Alex Okafor (Head of Product) advises, but the ARR number rolls into Maya's forecast |

## What Forecast Quality Looks Like

Forecast quality is reported quarterly. The key metric is **forecast call accuracy**: the absolute variance between the **week 8** commit forecast and the **closed-won** actual at quarter end. Our target is **within 5%**. Q1 2026 came in at 3.1%. **Q2 2026 came in at 14.5%**, the worst quarter we have had in two years, driven by the late-quarter Mid-Market dynamics not being reflected in the commit forecast until very late.

## Limits of the Methodology

Two structural limits are worth being explicit about:

1. **The forecast is dominated by Closed Won + Commit.** A late-cycle churn event in the existing book of business does not appear in `revenue.pipeline` and therefore does not move the forecast in the standard model. Renewals are tracked separately in the renewals desk's view and rolled into Jordan's contribution.
2. **Pipeline coverage is a sales-funnel metric.** High coverage tells you about new-logo health; it does not tell you about renewal health. Conflating the two is a common analyst mistake.

Both limits are visible in Q2 2026, the coverage stayed strong, but the renewal cohort dynamics did the damage.

wikiPages.title: Reporting Cadence & Stakeholder Map
wikiPages.body:
# Reporting Cadence & Stakeholder Map

This page documents the rhythm of revenue reporting at Helix Cloud, what gets published when, who owns each report, and which stakeholders are expected to read it. It is the operating calendar for the RevOps Analytics team and for any new analyst trying to understand the flow of information across the revenue org.

## Cadence at a Glance

| Cadence | Report | Owner | Primary audience |
|---|---|---|---|
| Daily | Pacing dashboard auto-refresh | Sam Iyer (RevOps) | Sales managers, Jordan Park |
| Weekly (Monday) | Monday metrics email | RevOps Analytics | Executives + sales/CS managers |
| Weekly (Friday) | Friday forecast email | Sam Iyer (RevOps) | Executives |
| Biweekly | RevOps stand-up | Sam Iyer | Sales managers, CS managers |
| Monthly | Monthly business review (MBR) | Maya Chen | Exec team + selected ICs |
| Quarterly | Board readout | Lindsey Park (with Maya, Sam) | Board of directors |

## Monday Metrics Email

The Monday metrics email is the most-read revenue artifact at Helix Cloud. It goes out by 09:00 PT every Monday and covers:

- ARR ending last week + week-over-week change
- Net new ARR week and quarter-to-date vs plan
- Closed-won by segment
- Pipeline coverage (Mid-Market, Enterprise, total)
- Top 5 deals committed for the week
- Renewals at risk (red and yellow health) for the next 60 days
- Open analytics questions

The email is written by the RevOps Analytics team, reviewed by Sam Iyer, and approved by Maya Chen before send. It is the place where the **first signal of a quarter going off-plan** typically surfaces.

## Friday Forecast Email

The Friday forecast email is shorter and goes only to executives (Lindsey, Maya, Marcus, Jordan, Priya, Alex, Sam). It contains:

- This week's commit forecast (closed + commit)
- This week's best-case forecast (closed + commit + best case)
- Week-over-week movement
- The two largest forecast changes (positive and negative) with names
- Pipeline coverage

Friday emails are archived in `revops-archive` Google Drive. Historical emails are useful when reconstructing how a quarter unfolded.

## Biweekly RevOps Stand-up

Every other Tuesday at 11:00 PT. 30 minutes. Standing agenda:

1. Forecast variance vs plan (5 min, Sam)
2. Pipeline health by segment (10 min, sales managers)
3. Renewals at risk (10 min, renewals desk + Jordan)
4. Open analytics requests (5 min)

The stand-up is the working session where most cross-functional renewal escalations get scoped. The RevOps Analytics team attends silently unless an action is requested.

## Monthly Business Review (MBR)

First Tuesday of every month at 10:00 PT. 90 minutes. Attendees: exec team + select managers. The MBR is the deepest internal revenue review and walks through:

- Month-end ARR by segment, product, and region
- Pipeline progression and conversion rates
- Health-score distribution
- Customer wins and losses with named accounts
- One topical deep-dive

The MBR is owned by Maya Chen and prepared by RevOps Analytics. The deep-dive topic rotates: pricing in one MBR, churn in another, expansion motion in a third. The **July 2026 MBR will feature a deep-dive on the Q2 2026 miss**, drawing on the diagnosis being assembled now.

## Quarterly Board Readout

The board readout happens 7–10 business days after quarter end. The next readout is **2026-07-15**. Format is:

- 30 minutes of revenue review (Maya leads, Lindsey opens)
- 15 minutes of product update (Alex)
- 15 minutes of platform / infra (Marcus)
- 30 minutes Q&A

The revenue review centers on a fixed deck template:

1. Headline ARR + net new ARR vs plan
2. Five-component breakdown vs plan
3. Segment view
4. Cohort retention (GRR / NRR rolling four-quarter)
5. Pipeline and forecast for the upcoming quarter
6. Drivers and recommended actions

The Q2 2026 readout will include a definitive diagnosis of the $1.9M variance. See [Q2 2026 Target & Actuals Memo](/wiki/q2-2026-target-and-actuals) for the data baseline.

## Stakeholder Map

Who needs what, when.

| Stakeholder | Role | What they read | Cadence |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lindsey Park** | CEO | Monday metrics, Friday forecast, MBR, board readout | All cadences |
| **Maya Chen** | VP Revenue (CRO) | All revenue reports + forecast pipeline detail | All cadences |
| **Marcus Chen** | CTO | MBR + board readout; not a regular reader of weekly | Monthly + quarterly |
| **Jordan Park** | VP CS | Monday metrics, renewals-at-risk view, MBR | Weekly + monthly |
| **Priya Rao** | Head of Marketing | Monday metrics, pipeline-coverage view, MBR | Weekly + monthly |
| **Alex Okafor** | Head of Product | Monday metrics (Lineage attach), product-usage rollup, MBR | Weekly + monthly |
| **Sam Iyer** | Director RevOps | Author of most of the above; owns analytics quality | All cadences |
| **Board of directors** | n/a | Board readout only, plus quarterly investor letter | Quarterly |
| **All-hands company** | n/a | Quarterly all-hands ARR slide | Quarterly |

## Crisp Rules for Analysts

A few rules that the analytics team has learned the hard way:

- **No surprises on Monday.** If a number in the Monday email will surprise an executive, give Maya a heads-up by Friday EOD.
- **Reconcile to ARR.** Any number you publish should reconcile to the ARR roll-forward in `revenue.events`. If it does not, the number is wrong or the definition is wrong.
- **Annotate methodology changes.** If a metric definition or filter changes, call it out in the email. Silent definition changes destroy trust.
- **Cite the source query.** Include the BigQuery query or Looker view link in any analysis document. See [BigQuery Conventions & Standard Queries](/wiki/bigquery-conventions).
- **Cross-link the personas.** When a question routes to a specific leader, send a note in their channel: Maya in #revenue-leadership, Jordan in #cs-leadership, Priya in #marketing-leads, Alex in #product, Sam in #revops-help. They expect direct outreach for anything blocking, not a forwarded email.

## What Lives Where

| Artifact | Location |
|---|---|
| Monday metrics email archive | `revops-archive` Google Drive, `Monday Metrics/` |
| Friday forecast archive | `revops-archive` Google Drive, `Friday Forecast/` |
| MBR decks | Executive Google Drive, `MBR/` |
| Board decks | Executive Google Drive, `Board Readouts/` (restricted) |
| BigQuery analyses | `revops-analytics` GitHub repo, `notebooks/` |
| Looker dashboards | `looker.helixcloud.com`, `Revenue/` folder |

If you cannot find something, post in #revops-help.
